proposals.title: Distinct Environmental Correlates of Walking versus Cycling for Transport among Adolescents: A Multi-Site Analysis of GPS and Accelerometer Data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on active transport often combines walking and cycling into a single 'active travel' outcome, assuming they share the same environmental determinants. This assumption may obscure specific, actionable policy and design interventions needed to promote each mode. For adolescents, whose independent mobility is crucial, understanding distinct environmental influences on walking and cycling is key to creating supportive neighbourhoods and increasing overall physical activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that built environment correlates (e.g., residential density, intersection density, land use mix, cycling infrastructure, and school walkability) will differ significantly between walking for transport and cycling for transport among adolescents, with cycling being more sensitive to dedicated infrastructure and longer trip distances.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of pooled, objectively measured data from pre-2026 international studies (e.g., IPEN Adolescent, iHealth) using GPS and accelerometry to identify trip purpose and mode. Using generalized linear mixed models (GLMMs) with city/site as a random effect, we will separately model the odds of walking trips and cycling trips as functions of GIS-derived environmental variables around home and school. We will compare model coefficients using Wald tests to formally assess differences in environmental correlates between the two modes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first multi-country, device-measured evidence on whether environmental predictors of adolescent active transport are mode-specific. It will advance ecological models of physical activity by clarifying the unique environmental needs for walking and cycling. Findings will inform more precise built environment interventions, such as prioritizing pedestrian connectivity for walking and protected cycle lanes for cycling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sallis and others has examined environmental correlates of overall active travel, this proposal explicitly tests the untested assumption of interchangeability. The novelty lies in the direct statistical comparison of mode-specific environmental associations using robust, pooled device-measured data from diverse settings.

proposals.title: A Cluster-Randomized Trial of Targeted Interventions to Increase Active Travel to School Among Adolescents with Distinct Behavioral and Perceptual Profiles
proposals.motivation: Active travel to school (ATS) is a key source of physical activity for adolescents, yet prevalence remains low. Pre-2026 research, including Sallis's IPEN Adolescent study, has identified distinct latent profiles of ATS behavior (e.g., 'perception-constrained', 'distance-constrained', 'habitual non-travelers') based on individual, social, and neighborhood factors. However, interventions are rarely tailored to these specific subgroups, likely limiting their effectiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: Interventions tailored to the specific barriers and facilitators characterizing pre-identified latent ATS profiles will be more effective at increasing ATS frequency and duration than a generic, one-size-fits-all intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-arm, cluster-randomized trial in 30 secondary schools. Schools will be randomized to: 1) a targeted intervention arm, where students are first classified into latent profiles via a validated survey and algorithm (e.g., Latent Profile Analysis using variables from IPEN), then receive a matched intervention (e.g., walkability audits for 'perception-constrained', walking school bus for 'distance-constrained', motivational messaging for 'habitual non-travelers'); 2) a generic ATS promotion arm; or 3) a wait-list control. The primary outcome is device-measured (accelerometer+GPS) ATS at 12-month follow-up. Analysis will use multilevel mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence on the efficacy of a precision public health approach to ATS promotion. It will move the field beyond identifying correlates to testing actionable, theory-informed interventions tailored to subgroup-specific mechanisms. Findings will offer a scalable model for translating latent class research into practice.
proposals.novelty: While latent profile analysis has been used descriptively in ATS research, this study innovatively uses pre-identified profiles as the basis for assigning different intervention components within the same trial, testing a personalized intervention logic model.

proposals.title: Developing a Sequence-Optimized Predictive Audit (SOPA): Using Whole-Day Activity Profiles and Machine Learning to Create a Brief, Empirically Validated Neighborhood Audit Tool for Youth
proposals.motivation: Promoting active travel to school (ATS) is a key public health strategy, but existing neighborhood audit tools are often lengthy or based on generic associations with a single behavior. Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others shows that ATS is embedded within broader daily routines, and that device-measured physical activity patterns are associated with neighborhood walkability. A more efficient, theory-informed audit tool is needed to identify specific environmental features that support not just ATS, but the complex, activity-rich daily sequences that characterize healthy youth mobility.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, modifiable neighborhood audit items can be identified that are predictive of whole-day activity profiles characterized by active commuting embedded within other supportive routines, enabling the creation of a brief, sequence-optimized audit tool with stronger predictive validity for healthy mobility patterns than tools derived from single-behavior models.
proposals.method: We will use GPS and accelerometer data from existing studies (e.g., IPEN Adolescent) to construct whole-day activity sequences (location, timing, intensity) for adolescents. Optimal Matching or similar sequence analysis will classify profiles, identifying 'optimal' sequences with ATS and high activity. Using audit data (e.g., from MAPS or similar tools), we will train a Random Forest or Lasso regression model to predict profile class membership. The model will identify the minimal set of audit items (e.g., sidewalk continuity, pedestrian signals, park proximity) most predictive of optimal sequences.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the SOPA, a novel, brief audit tool whose items are empirically selected for predicting healthy daily mobility patterns, not just ATS. It advances methodological fusion in health behavior research by linking sequence analysis with predictive modeling for tool development. The tool will provide a practical resource for planners and researchers to efficiently assess neighborhoods for youth activity support.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond correlating environments with single behaviors (e.g., ATS) or total activity to identify environmental predictors of complex, temporally ordered daily routines. The fusion of sequence analysis for outcome definition with machine learning for audit item selection represents a novel, data-driven approach to creating parsimonious measurement tools.

proposals.title: Validating Crowdsourced Pedestrian Networks with GPS-Linked Stepping Bouts: A Novel Method for Assessing Neighbourhood Walkability and Adolescent Active Travel
proposals.motivation: Accurate pedestrian infrastructure data is critical for walkability research and urban planning, yet crowdsourced maps like OpenStreetMap are not systematically validated against actual pedestrian behavior. This gap limits the precision of studies linking built environment features to physical activity, especially for adolescents whose active travel to school and local destinations is a key health behavior. A method to ground-truth mapped paths with objective movement data is needed to improve environmental exposure measures.
proposals.hypothesis: Algorithmically linking high-fidelity activPAL stepping bouts with concurrent GPS data will reveal systematic discrepancies between crowdsourced pedestrian networks and actual walking routes, and these validated network layers will show stronger associations with adolescent active travel outcomes than standard mapped networks.
proposals.method: We will use existing data from studies like IPEN Adolescent, where participants wear activPAL devices and GPS loggers. Stepping bouts will be identified from activPAL events and spatiotemporally linked to GPS points. A map-matching algorithm (e.g., based on Hidden Markov Models) will assign these GPS-linked stepping bouts to pedestrian paths in OpenStreetMap. Paths with no matched stepping over a defined threshold will be flagged as potentially inaccurate or inaccessible, while frequent stepping clusters unmatched to any path will indicate missing infrastructure. The resulting validated network will be compared to the raw OSM network in models predicting active travel to school.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, behaviorally-validated pedestrian network layer that directly links infrastructure data to real-world walking performance. It will provide a new methodological tool for urban planning and public health research, improving the measurement of environmental exposures for physical activity. The findings will offer concrete evidence for map corrections and infrastructure investments to support adolescent active travel.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have associated walkability metrics with activity, this method innovates by fusing high-resolution stepping and location data to validate the existence and use of specific mapped paths, moving from ecological correlations to direct, path-level behavioral validation of geographic data.

proposals.title: Developing a City-wide School Walkability Support Index: An Ecological Health Assessment Framework for Benchmarking and Prioritization
proposals.motivation: Despite known associations between neighbourhood walkability and adolescent active travel, public health and planning agencies lack standardized, scalable tools to systematically assess and compare the environmental support for walking to school across an entire city. This limits the ability to equitably prioritize infrastructure investments and track progress toward creating healthier urban environments for youth. A city-wide benchmarking system is urgently needed to translate research into actionable policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a standardized ecological health assessment framework—where core environmental indicators are systematically measured and aggregated into a composite index—will yield a valid, reliable, and policy-relevant School Walkability Support Index capable of identifying 'walkability deserts' and enabling longitudinal tracking of the built environment's support for active travel to school.
proposals.method: We will define a core set of infrastructural and safety indicators (e.g., sidewalk completeness, crossing safety, traffic speed, connectivity) based on existing audit tools (e.g., MAPS, NEWS) and expert consensus. Using GIS and field audits, these indicators will be measured within a standardized network buffer (e.g., 1km) around every public school in a target city. Following ecological index methodologies, indicators will be normalized, weighted via expert Delphi panel, and aggregated using a multi-criteria decision analysis algorithm (e.g., weighted linear combination) to compute a composite School Walkability Support Index score (0-100) for each school.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first operational, city-wide benchmarking tool for school walkability, directly enabling cross-sectional comparisons and longitudinal monitoring. It will contribute a novel methodological framework, borrowed from environmental science, for translating discrete built environment audits into a composite index for public health and urban planning decision-making.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit conceptual and methodological analogy to ecological health assessments, moving beyond single-audit or regression-based studies to create a standardized, comparative index system for city-wide environmental benchmarking related to adolescent active travel.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Micro-Accessibility: A Participatory Sensor-Augmented Audit Framework for School-Active Travel Routes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including IPEN Adolescent studies, shows neighbourhood walkability is associated with higher physical activity, yet standard audits often miss micro-scale barriers (e.g., cracked pavements, narrow crossings) critical for adolescents with disabilities or mobility aids. This creates a disparity between technical walkability scores and the lived experience of accessibility, potentially limiting active travel to school for vulnerable groups. Ground-truthing these environments with those most affected is essential for equitable built environment interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Micro-scale environmental barriers identified through a participatory, sensor-augmented audit framework will show significant discrepancies from standard expert audits, and these discrepancies will be more strongly associated with self-reported difficulty and avoidance of active school travel among adolescents with mobility challenges than traditional walkability metrics.
proposals.method: We will recruit adolescents who use mobility aids (e.g., walkers, wheelchairs) and their parents from schools in varying walkability areas. Participants will be trained as co-researchers and equipped with low-cost sensor kits (accelerometers, GPS) on their aids to log objective trip data (vibration, slope, stops). A companion smartphone app will use ecological momentary assessment (EMA) for low-burden subjective feedback on perceived safety and difficulty at triggered locations. Data fusion will use geographic information systems (GIS) to create layered 'lived experience maps,' and statistical analyses (e.g., multilevel modelling) will test associations between sensor/EMA-identified barriers and device-measured active travel patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, community-validated methodology for micro-scale accessibility auditing that centers lived experience. It will generate new evidence on the specific environmental features that constrain active travel for adolescents with mobility challenges, moving beyond aggregate walkability scores. The resulting framework and maps will provide actionable data for urban planners and school districts to prioritize targeted, inclusive route improvements.
proposals.novelty: While participatory mapping and sensor-based audits exist separately, this study's integration of objective sensor data from mobility aids with real-time subjective app feedback in a co-design framework with adolescents is novel. It directly tests the validity of standard audit tools against a ground-truthed benchmark of usability, advancing methods in health geography and disability studies.

proposals.title: Disentangling Perceived and Objective Walkability: A Multi-Method Study of Associations with Device-Measured Physical Activity in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's IPEN Adolescent study, consistently shows associations between perceived neighborhood environments and adolescent active travel. However, reliance on self-reported environmental data introduces potential bias and limits causal inference. Combining these subjective measures with objective, GIS-derived built environment data is critical to identify which specific, modifiable environmental features most strongly influence adolescent physical activity patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that objective GIS measures of walkability (e.g., intersection density, land use mix) will have stronger associations with device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) in adolescents than self-reported environmental perceptions, and that the congruence or discrepancy between perceived and objective measures will further explain activity patterns.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing cohorts (e.g., IPEN Adolescent) with device-measured activity (ActiGraph) and perceived environment surveys (Neighborhood Environment Walkability Scale - Youth). For each participant's geocoded home and school address, we will calculate objective walkability indices using GIS databases (e.g., OpenStreetMap, municipal data). Core algorithms will compute net residential density, intersection density, land use mix entropy, and transit stop density within network buffers. Statistical analyses will employ generalized linear mixed models to test independent and interactive associations of perceived and objective measures with MVPA, controlling for demographic covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a clearer, evidence-based picture of which built environment attributes are most consequential for adolescent activity, moving beyond perceptions to actionable urban design metrics. It will directly inform more precise policy and planning interventions aimed at increasing physical activity through neighborhood and school siting decisions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used GIS or audits, few studies have simultaneously modeled the independent and interactive effects of both perceived and objective walkability measures on device-measured activity in an international adolescent sample, allowing for a direct comparison of their predictive utility.

proposals.title: Beyond the Buffer: Comparing Fixed-Neighborhood and GPS-Derived Personal Activity Space Measures of Walkability for Adolescent Physical Activity
proposals.motivation: Research on built environment and adolescent physical activity (PA) predominantly uses fixed geographic buffers (e.g., 1 km) around homes or schools as the assumed exposure area. This static approach may misrepresent the environments adolescents actually experience, as their daily mobility patterns create unique 'personal activity spaces'. This mismatch could explain inconsistent findings in the literature and limit the effectiveness of environmental interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that walkability metrics derived from GPS-based personal activity spaces will show stronger associations with device-measured physical activity in adolescents than traditional walkability metrics calculated from fixed 1 km home-centered buffers.
proposals.method: We will recruit adolescents (n=250, aged 12-17) and collect seven days of GPS and accelerometer data. For each participant, we will calculate two exposure measures: 1) a traditional walkability index (residential density, land use mix, intersection density) within a 1 km street-network buffer from home, and 2) a GPS-derived activity space using a standard deviational ellipse or daily path area. Walkability will be calculated within this personalized polygon. Associations between each walkability measure and daily moderate-to-vigorous PA (MVPA) will be tested using multilevel linear regression, adjusting for demographics and school clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the validity of fixed buffers versus dynamic exposure measures for adolescent PA research. It will advance methodological precision in environmental epidemiology and inform more accurate policy and planning for creating activity-supportive environments tailored to how adolescents actually move through space.
proposals.novelty: While personal activity spaces are used in some environmental studies, their direct comparison to fixed buffers for walkability exposure, specifically in an adolescent sample using device-based PA and location data, represents a novel test of a fundamental methodological assumption in the field.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Walkability Composite: A Principal Component and Causal Mediation Analysis of Urban Form Patterns and Adolescent Active Travel
proposals.motivation: Composite walkability indices are widely used in urban health research, but they risk masking the specific built environment features that drive physical activity, particularly among adolescents. This 'black box' approach limits the translation of research into precise, actionable urban design guidelines. Drawing on successful methods from nutritional epidemiology, which disentangle dietary patterns, this study aims to reveal the mechanistic drivers within walkable environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that principal component analysis will identify distinct urban form patterns (e.g., 'highly connected retail' vs. 'park-oriented residential'), and that causal mediation analysis will reveal that the association between a given pattern and higher levels of adolescent active travel is significantly mediated by a single, specific built environment component, such as intersection density.
proposals.method: Using pre-existing GIS data and device-measured physical activity data from the IPEN Adolescent study, we will first perform principal component analysis (PCA) on standardized metrics of intersection density, land use mix, retail density, and park proximity. The resulting component scores will represent distinct urban form patterns. We will then employ causal mediation analysis (using the 'mediation' package in R) to formally test whether the total effect of a pattern on utilitarian walking is significantly mediated by a specific built environment variable, controlling for key confounders like age, sex, and socioeconomic status.
proposals.contribution: This research will move the field beyond composite scores by identifying which specific, modifiable features of walkable neighborhoods are the most potent drivers of adolescent active travel. The findings will provide stronger, more targeted evidence for urban planners and public health officials seeking to design activity-promoting environments. Methodologically, it will demonstrate the utility of a causal mediation framework for unpacking the 'active ingredients' of complex environmental exposures.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying the pattern decomposition and causal mediation techniques common in nutritional epidemiology to the built environment, directly addressing the long-standing critique that composite walkability scores obscure mechanistic pathways. It shifts the analytical focus from association to mediation for specific environmental features.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Walkability Composite: Differential Associations of Intersection Density, Land Use Mix, and Residential Density with Device-Measured Physical Activity and Sedentary Time in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's IPEN Adolescent studies, consistently links neighborhood walkability composites to physical activity. However, these composites (e.g., combining intersection density, land use mix, residential density) may obscure the unique effects of specific built environment features. For targeted urban planning and public health interventions, it is critical to understand which components are most strongly associated with specific activity domains (e.g., transport walking, leisure-time activity, sedentary bouts) in adolescent populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual components of walkability will show distinct, non-uniform associations with device-measured physical activity and sedentary time patterns in adolescents, with intersection density most strongly associated with transport-related stepping and land use mix associated with a broader range of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of existing, pre-2026 IPEN Adolescent cohort data, using accelerometer and GPS data from adolescents in multiple countries. Geographic Information Systems (GIS) will be used to calculate individual walkability components (intersection density, land use mix entropy, residential density, net retail area ratio) within network buffers of home and school addresses. We will employ multivariate linear and compositional data analysis regression models, adjusting for demographic covariates and school-level clustering, to test associations between each standardized component and outcomes including total stepping, stepping within transport time windows, and sedentary time.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond composite scores by providing actionable evidence on which specific built environment features are linked to different activity behaviors. The findings will inform more precise policy and urban design recommendations aimed at increasing adolescent physical activity and reducing sedentary time through targeted environmental modifications.
proposals.novelty: While composite walkability indices are common, this study explicitly tests the component-level effects using robust, device-measured activity data across multiple domains and in an international adolescent sample, addressing a significant gap in environmental health research identified in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Disentangling Causality: A Longitudinal and Quasi-Experimental Analysis of Self-Selection vs. Environmental Determinism in Adolescent Physical Activity and Neighbourhood Walkability
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 cross-sectional studies consistently show associations between neighbourhood walkability and higher physical activity, but the causal direction remains ambiguous. This is a critical gap for public health policy, as the assumption of environmental determinism underpins costly built-environment interventions. If self-selection of active individuals into walkable areas is the primary driver, the efficacy of such interventions is questionable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion, and potentially all, of the observed cross-sectional association between neighbourhood walkability and device-measured physical activity in adolescents is attributable to residential self-selection by their parents, rather than a direct causal effect of the environment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal analysis of adolescents (aged 12-16 at baseline) who move residences, using a quasi-experimental, difference-in-differences design. Baseline and follow-up (2-year) data will include device-measured daily stepping (ActiGraph accelerometers) and detailed GIS-based walkability indices (net residential density, land use mix, intersection density). We will employ propensity score matching to create a control group of non-movers with similar baseline characteristics. The primary analysis will use multilevel mixed-effects models to compare changes in activity for movers to walkable vs. less-walkable areas against the control group, adjusting for parental preferences for walkability (measured via validated pre-move surveys).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the strongest pre-2026 evidence to date on the causal pathways linking walkability and activity in adolescents. It will directly quantify the relative contributions of self-selection and environmental exposure, offering crucial evidence for urban planning and public health investment. The findings will refine theoretical models of ecological influences on behaviour by explicitly modelling preference-based sorting.
proposals.novelty: While self-selection is acknowledged as a confounder in adult studies, it is rarely the central focus and has not been rigorously tested in adolescent populations using longitudinal, quasi-experimental designs with objective activity measures. This study inverts the standard analytical frame to test self-selection as a primary explanatory mechanism.

proposals.title: Context-Aware Ecological Momentary Assessment of Adolescent Walking Experiences: Linking Real-Time Perceptions to GIS-Based Walkability
proposals.motivation: Understanding how built environments influence physical activity requires linking objective measures of the environment with real-time subjective experiences. Traditional surveys suffer from recall bias, and random Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) prompts often miss the specific activity episodes of interest, particularly for adolescents whose walking is sporadic. A method that captures perceptions precisely during walking bouts in specific locations is needed to advance the study of environment-behavior interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents' real-time perceptions of stress, enjoyment, and safety during walking will be more strongly associated with GIS-derived neighborhood walkability indices when measured via context-aware triggering compared to associations derived from traditional recall-based surveys or randomly timed EMA.
proposals.method: We will develop a smartphone app using the open-source movisensXS platform. The app will use the phone's inertial sensors and a validated algorithm (e.g., a machine learning classifier trained on accelerometer data to detect walking) to passively identify the start of a walking bout. Upon detection, the app will cross-reference the GPS location with a pre-loaded GIS database of walkability (e.g., using the 3Ds: density, diversity, design). If the bout occurs in a pre-defined area of interest (e.g., home neighborhood, school environs), it will trigger an EMA prompt asking about current perceptions. We will recruit 100 adolescents for a 7-day monitoring period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel methodological framework for capturing in-situ perceptual data aligned with specific micro-environments and activities. It will yield more precise estimates of how objective walkability influences momentary affective and cognitive states during walking. The findings will inform more targeted interventions to promote active travel by identifying which environmental features are most closely linked to positive walking experiences for youth.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the automated, context-aware triggering of EMA prompts based on passive activity and location sensing, moving beyond random or scheduled prompts. This creates a direct, real-time link between the objective environmental exposure (GIS walkability) and the subjective experience, a methodological advance for health behavior and built environment research.

proposals.title: Temporal Dynamics of Walkability: A Multi-Modal Data Integration System for Modeling Adolescent Pedestrian Experience
proposals.motivation: Existing walkability audits, often based on static imagery or GIS data, fail to capture the dynamic, experiential factors that influence pedestrian safety and behavior, especially for adolescents. This static view limits our understanding of how walkability varies by time of day, traffic conditions, and perceived safety, which are critical for promoting active travel to school and in neighborhoods. A more nuanced, temporally-sensitive measure is needed to inform urban planning that genuinely supports adolescent physical activity.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating static built environment features from street view imagery with dynamic, experiential data streams will produce a temporally-variable walkability score that is more strongly associated with observed adolescent pedestrian activity patterns than traditional static measures.
proposals.method: We will develop a system integrating: 1) Computer vision analysis of Google Street View imagery using pre-trained convolutional neural networks (e.g., ResNet-50) to extract perceptual features (e.g., sidewalk quality, street enclosure). 2) Dynamic data streams including real-time traffic volume/speed from municipal sensors, historical pedestrian-vehicle incident reports, and anonymized, aggregated movement data from mobile devices (subject to strict ethical protocols). 3) A machine learning model (e.g., gradient boosting) will fuse these multi-modal inputs to generate hourly walkability scores for street segments, validated against device-measured stepping data from adolescent cohorts in existing IPEN studies.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide urban planners and public health researchers with a validated, high-resolution tool for auditing walkability that accounts for real-world variability in safety and experience. It will advance the methodological integration of perceptual and dynamic data in environmental health sciences. The findings will offer novel evidence on how temporal environmental dynamics influence adolescent active travel behaviors.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond cross-sectional, imagery-only audits by creating a fused, time-sensitive model of walkability. It uniquely combines computer vision-derived perceptual features with real-time behavioral and safety data streams to model the pedestrian experience dynamically, a significant advance over static GIS-based or audit-tool measures prevalent in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Causal Mediation of Adolescent Health: Testing Stepping Patterns as a Mechanism Linking Neighborhood Walkability to Cardiometabolic Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence from IPEN Adolescent and similar studies shows cross-sectional associations between neighborhood walkability and adolescent physical activity, but the causal pathways to health outcomes remain unclear. Establishing whether objectively measured stepping patterns mediate the effect of walkability on health is critical for designing effective, evidence-based environmental interventions to combat rising adolescent obesity and cardiometabolic risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the causal effect of neighborhood walkability on adolescent cardiometabolic health (e.g., BMI, waist circumference, blood pressure) is mediated by changes in daily stepping patterns, specifically higher volumes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) accumulated in bouts.
proposals.method: We will apply a formal causal mediation analysis framework, using the interventional (inverse odds ratio weighting) approach to handle exposure-mediator interaction and potential mediator-outcome confounding. Data will be harmonized from the IPEN Adolescent study (pre-2026), including GIS-based walkability indices, 7-day accelerometer-derived stepping patterns (total steps, MVPA steps), and health biomarkers. The analysis will estimate natural direct and indirect effects, with sensitivity analyses for unmeasured confounding of the mediator-outcome relationship, as per VanderWeele (2015) and methods implemented in the 'mediation' R package.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond association to provide the first causal-mediation evidence on *how* walkable environments improve adolescent health. It will quantify the proportion of the total effect operating through stepping behavior, informing whether interventions should prioritize environmental redesign, activity promotion, or both. The findings will strengthen the theoretical basis for using urban planning as a public health strategy.
proposals.novelty: While Sallis and colleagues have documented associations, this proposal applies a formal, robust causal mediation framework—a significant methodological advance in the field—to disentangle mechanisms using existing high-quality, device-measured longitudinal data on adolescents, a key life stage for intervention.

proposals.title: Resilience as a Buffer: Testing Effect Modification of Personal Factors on the Association Between Neighborhood Walkability and Device-Measured Sleep in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's IPEN Adolescent work, robustly links neighborhood environments to physical activity but less consistently to adolescent sleep, a critical health behavior. Adverse neighborhood factors (e.g., low walkability, safety concerns) are posited to disrupt sleep, yet outcomes vary, suggesting unexamined protective factors. Investigating personal resilience—an individual's capacity to adapt to stress—as a potential moderator addresses a key gap in understanding for whom neighborhood characteristics matter most for sleep health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher levels of individual resilience factors (e.g., self-efficacy, coping skills, perceived social support) will buffer (weaken) the negative association between adverse neighborhood walkability/safety and objective measures of sleep duration and quality in adolescents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis using existing data from studies like IPEN Adolescent, integrating GPS/GIS-derived neighborhood walkability and safety metrics with 7-day accelerometer-measured sleep outcomes (duration, efficiency, timing). Resilience will be assessed via validated pre-2026 scales (e.g., Child and Youth Resilience Measure). The core analysis employs hierarchical linear regression with interaction terms (Neighborhood Index × Resilience Score), adjusting for age, sex, socioeconomic status, and school clustering. Simple slopes analysis will probe significant interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will advance the socio-ecological model by explicitly testing a person-environment interaction for a novel outcome (sleep), moving beyond main effects. It will provide evidence for targeted interventions, suggesting that enhancing personal resilience may protect sleep health in suboptimal environments, complementing place-based changes.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sallis's prior work examines neighborhood associations with activity and diet, applying an effect modification framework to sleep via resilience is novel. It shifts the focus from solely blaming environments to identifying protective individual characteristics that may explain heterogeneity in adolescent health outcomes.

proposals.title: Pathways from Neighborhood Disorder to Adolescent Sleep Health: Mediating Roles of Perceived Stress and Device-Measured Physical Activity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others has established that neighborhood environments are associated with adolescent physical activity and health behaviors. However, the mechanisms linking neighborhood disorder (e.g., crime, noise, vandalism) to objectively measured sleep outcomes in adolescents remain poorly understood. This gap is critical because poor sleep in adolescence is a known risk factor for mental and physical health problems, and modifiable environmental pathways could inform targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the negative association between perceived neighborhood disorder and adolescent sleep duration and efficiency is serially mediated by higher perceived psychological stress and lower daily moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA).
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis of approximately 500 adolescents (aged 12-17) from the IPEN Adolescent study sites, leveraging existing GIS and perceived environmental data. Sleep duration, efficiency, and physical activity will be measured concurrently using 7-day wrist-worn accelerometry (ActiGraph GT3X+). Perceived stress will be assessed via the Perceived Stress Scale. The primary analysis will use a path analysis framework within a structural equation modeling (SEM) approach in Mplus, testing the specified serial mediation model while controlling for key covariates (age, sex, socioeconomic status, school location). Model fit will be evaluated using CFI, TLI, and RMSEA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated test of psychosocial and behavioral pathways linking neighborhood disorder to objective sleep metrics in adolescents. It will advance the field by moving beyond simple associations to elucidate causal mechanisms, directly informing the design of multi-level interventions that could target stress reduction or activity promotion to mitigate environmental impacts on sleep.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked environments to activity or sleep separately, this proposal uniquely integrates these domains and posits a specific, testable serial mediation pathway. It applies Sallis's expertise in ecological models and device-based measurement to the understudied outcome of adolescent sleep health, a novel synthesis of his pre-2026 research themes.

proposals.title: Associations between neighbourhood walkability, school schedules, and a data-driven multidimensional sleep health composite in adolescents: a cross-sectional and longitudinal analysis
proposals.motivation: Multidimensional sleep health (MDSH) composites, which combine dimensions like duration, timing, and efficiency, are gaining traction as holistic predictors of adolescent well-being. However, existing composites often use arbitrary, equal weighting schemes, potentially obscuring which dimensions are most critical for health. Furthermore, pre-2026 research by Sallis and others highlights neighbourhood walkability and school start times as key environmental correlates of physical activity, but their specific associations with a refined MDSH composite remain unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite sleep health score derived using data-driven weighting (e.g., principal component analysis) will show stronger associations with adolescent mental health and academic outcomes than an equally weighted composite. We further hypothesize that higher neighbourhood walkability and later school start times will be independently associated with more favourable scores on this optimized composite.
proposals.method: Using existing accelerometer and survey data from cohorts like IPEN Adolescent, we will derive device-measured sleep dimensions (duration, timing, regularity, efficiency). We will develop alternative composite scores: 1) an equally weighted standard, and 2) weights derived from Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on health-relevant correlates. We will compare these composites using generalized linear mixed models, testing associations with mental health (survey) and neighbourhood walkability (GIS-derived index) cross-sectionally, and with school start times longitudinally across academic years, adjusting for key covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, empirically weighted MDSH composite for use in adolescent research, moving the field beyond arbitrary metrics. It will directly integrate sleep health with Dr. Sallis's established research themes by quantifying how modifiable built environment (walkability) and policy (school timing) factors are associated with this optimized health composite.
proposals.novelty: This proposal innovatively applies data-driven weighting, common in other fields, to adolescent sleep composite validation. It uniquely bridges the environmental determinants of physical activity (walkability, school context) with the emerging paradigm of multidimensional sleep health, testing novel hypotheses about their interrelationships.

proposals.title: Micro-Route Environmental Exposure and Adolescent Dietary Behaviors: A GPS and GIS-Based Analysis of Daily Paths to School
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Sallis's IPEN Adolescent study, shows neighborhood walkability and food environments are associated with adolescent health behaviors. However, these studies typically rely on static residential or school buffers, which may misrepresent the environments adolescents are actually exposed to during daily travel. Understanding the specific micro-routes and the food outlets passed en route to school is critical for designing targeted interventions to influence dietary choices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that greater exposure to unhealthy food outlets (e.g., fast food, convenience stores) along an adolescent's actual GPS-tracked route to school will be associated with a higher frequency of unhealthy dietary purchases and consumption, independent of the home and school neighborhood food environment.
proposals.method: We will recruit adolescents (n=200, aged 13-15) and use GPS devices (e.g., QStarz BT-Q1000XT) to track their routes to/from school for one week. GPS tracks will be processed in GIS (e.g., ArcGIS) using path segmentation algorithms (e.g., ST-DBSCAN for stop detection) and snapped to street networks. Food outlet data will be audited and geocoded. Exposure will be calculated using dynamic buffers (e.g., 50m) around the actual route path. Associations between route-based exposure metrics and device-measured physical activity (ActiGraph accelerometers) and survey-reported dietary behaviors will be tested using multilevel regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond static neighborhood definitions to provide novel evidence on how microenvironmental exposures during daily travel influence adolescent behavior. It will contribute a new methodology for integrating high-resolution GPS, GIS, and audit data to define personal activity spaces. Findings will inform more precise public health policies, such as zoning around school routes and 'healthy corridor' initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies associate home or school neighborhoods with behavior, this proposal innovates by analyzing the specific, GPS-defined route as the primary unit of exposure assessment. It directly links real-time path data with the food environment to test a more precise mechanism of environmental influence on adolescent diet.

proposals.title: Replacing Sitting with Light Walking versus Standing: A Comparative Analysis of Cardiometabolic Benefits in Adolescents Using Device-Based Measures
proposals.motivation: Excessive sedentary time, particularly prolonged sitting, is a well-established risk factor for poor cardiometabolic health in youth. While replacing sitting with standing is beneficial, pre-2026 evidence suggests light-intensity physical activity (LPA) may offer superior metabolic advantages. This study directly addresses a critical gap by comparing these two specific behavioral substitutions within the daily context of adolescents, a population where habitual activity patterns are formed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, compared to standing, replacing sitting time with light walking will be associated with significantly greater improvements in cardiometabolic risk markers (e.g., waist circumference, fasting insulin, HDL cholesterol) among adolescents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis using existing data from studies like IPEN Adolescent, focusing on participants with concurrent accelerometry and cardiometabolic biomarker data. The primary exposure will be device-measured time spent in sitting, standing (using inclinometry), and light walking (defined as 100-1999 counts per minute). We will employ isotemporal substitution models within multivariate linear regression to estimate the association of replacing 30 minutes of sitting with 30 minutes of either standing or light walking with cardiometabolic outcomes, adjusting for school and neighborhood walkability (GIS-derived), moderate-to-vigorous activity, and sociodemographics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct, quantitative comparison of the cardiometabolic value of two feasible, real-world alternatives to sitting in adolescents. It will move the field beyond generic 'reduce sitting' messages by offering evidence-based, prioritized behavioral targets for public health interventions aimed at improving youth metabolic health through daily activity reallocation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sallis and others has established associations between neighborhood walkability and activity patterns, this proposal innovatively applies isotemporal substitution modeling to directly contrast two specific behavioral substitutions (light walking vs. standing) within the same analytical framework, leveraging precise device-based postural and movement data.

proposals.title: Do sit-stand desks increase adolescent non-sedentary time without requiring self-monitoring? A school-based, device-measured intervention study.
proposals.motivation: School-based environmental interventions, like sit-stand desks, aim to reduce adolescent sedentary time, but their real-world efficacy is unclear. Many successful behavior change interventions rely on conscious self-monitoring, which is burdensome and may not be sustainable. It is critical to test if passive environmental modifications alone can produce meaningful, lasting changes in device-measured activity, aligning with ecological models of health behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the introduction of sit-stand desks in secondary school classrooms will lead to a significant increase in device-measured non-sedentary time (standing + stepping) during school hours, and that this increase will be achieved without participants engaging in formal self-monitoring of their behavior.
proposals.method: We propose a cluster-randomized controlled trial in 8 secondary schools (4 intervention, 4 control) over one academic year. Intervention classrooms will be fitted with height-adjustable sit-stand desks. The primary outcome is school-time non-sedentary time, measured objectively using thigh-worn activPAL4 micro devices for 7 consecutive days at baseline, mid-term (3 months), and post-intervention (9 months). Crucially, the intervention protocol will explicitly exclude any instruction for students to track or log their desk usage or activity. Analysis will use multilevel linear mixed models, adjusting for school-level clustering, baseline activity, and relevant covariates (e.g., gender, BMI).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, device-measured evidence on the effectiveness of a specific environmental modification in a real-world school setting. It will directly inform public health and educational policy by testing the practical premise that environmental changes can yield benefits without placing additional cognitive burden (self-monitoring) on adolescents.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sallis and others has established associations between environments and activity, this study uniquely isolates the effect of a discrete environmental change from the concurrent use of behavioral self-regulation strategies, a key untested assumption in many intervention designs.

proposals.title: Neighbourhood walkability and device-measured sedentary time in adults with cardiometabolic conditions: A cross-sectional and intervention pilot study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence shows neighbourhood walkability is associated with physical activity and sedentary patterns in adolescents and the general population. However, adults with diagnosed cardiometabolic conditions (e.g., type 2 diabetes, hypertension) are at higher risk from prolonged sedentary time, yet it is unknown how their sedentary behaviour is shaped by neighbourhood environments. This gap limits the development of targeted environmental and behavioural interventions for this at-risk group.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher neighbourhood walkability will be associated with lower device-measured total sedentary time and more frequent sedentary breaks in adults with cardiometabolic conditions. Further, we hypothesize that a behavioural intervention focused on local walkability audits and route planning will reduce sedentary time more than a standard health education control.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-phase study. First, a cross-sectional analysis of 300 adults with physician-diagnosed cardiometabolic conditions, using thigh-worn activPAL4 devices to measure sedentary time (sitting/lying) and breaks over 7 days. Neighbourhood walkability will be assessed using the established Walk Score® and a geographic information systems (GIS)-based walkability index (integrating net residential density, land use mix, intersection density, and retail floor area ratio). Associations will be tested using generalized linear mixed models, adjusting for individual and household covariates. Second, a 12-week pilot RCT (n=60) will test a 'Neighbourhood Mobility' intervention, where participants use a tablet-based app to audit local walkability barriers/facilitators and co-plan active errands with a counsellor, versus a control group receiving generic activity pamphlets.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence on how objective neighbourhood walkability relates to device-measured sedentary behaviour in a clinical population. It will pilot a novel, environmentally-informed intervention model that moves beyond generic advice, directly addressing the person-environment interaction. Findings will inform the design of larger-scale public health and clinical strategies to reduce sedentary time through environmental and behavioural levers.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sallis's IPEN Adolescent work examined walkability and active travel, this proposal uniquely applies the framework to sedentary behaviour in a clinical adult population, combining rigorous device-based measurement with a targeted behavioural-environmental intervention pilot, which is novel for cardiometabolic disease management.

proposals.title: Determining the Minimal Effective Dose of Daily Sit-to-Stand Transitions for Metabolic Health in Adolescents: A School and Neighborhood Context Analysis
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is an established risk factor for poor metabolic health in youth, independent of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity. While breaking up sitting with standing or light activity is beneficial, public health guidelines lack specific, actionable thresholds for sit-to-stand transitions. Determining a minimal effective daily 'dose' would provide a clear, measurable target for interventions, particularly in school and home settings where sedentary time is high.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there is a non-linear dose-response relationship between daily sit-to-stand transitions and fasting insulin/glucose levels, and that achieving a threshold of ≥60 transitions per day will be associated with clinically meaningful improvements in metabolic markers, independent of overall physical activity. We further hypothesize that school and neighborhood walkability will moderate this relationship.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 7-day accelerometer-based study in 300 adolescents (aged 12-16), using thigh-worn activPAL devices to precisely measure sit-to-stand transitions. Fasting blood samples will assess insulin, glucose, and lipids. Using generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs), we will model the non-linear dose-response curve between daily transition counts and metabolic outcomes. Breakpoint analysis will identify potential threshold values. Multi-level models will then test if school environment (policies) and GIS-derived neighborhood walkability (using IPEN Adolescent methods) moderate the dose-response relationship.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence-based, quantitative target for sit-to-stand transitions in adolescents, moving beyond generic 'break up sitting' advice. It will directly inform the development of targeted school-based interventions and public health guidelines. Furthermore, by integrating environmental moderators, it will identify contexts where such interventions may be most effective or necessary.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sallis and others has established associations between environment, behavior, and health, this study uniquely applies a clinical 'dose-response' framework to a specific, device-measured sedentary behavior metric. It moves from association to prescription by seeking to identify a minimal effective dose, a novel step in behavioral epidemiology.

proposals.title: A Context-Aware Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention to Reduce Prolonged Sitting in Adolescents: Integrating Real-Time Location and Device-Measured Activity
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a significant health risk for adolescents, independent of physical activity levels. Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others has established strong associations between neighborhood and school environments and activity patterns, but interventions to disrupt sitting in real-time are lacking. Mobile health (mHealth) Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (JITAIs) offer a promising, scalable approach to deliver personalized behavior change prompts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a JITAI delivering personalized prompts for sitting breaks, based on real-time context (location, time, and prior activity), will significantly reduce device-measured prolonged sitting bouts and increase light-intensity activity breaks in adolescents, compared to a generic, time-based prompt intervention.
proposals.method: We will recruit adolescents (n=120) and use a micro-randomized trial design. Participants will wear a thigh-worn accelerometer (e.g., activPAL) and carry a GPS-enabled smartphone for 4 weeks. A custom mobile app will implement a JITAI algorithm that uses real-time sensor data. The decision algorithm will be based on a reinforcement learning framework, where prompts to stand/move are delivered when a prolonged sitting bout is detected (>30 mins) AND the individual is in a context deemed 'opportune' (e.g., at school between classes, at home during non-study hours, but not during transit). The control condition will deliver generic time-based prompts. The primary outcome is the number of prolonged sitting bouts (>30 min) per day.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel evidence on the efficacy of a context-aware JITAI for reducing adolescent sitting. It will extend Sallis's ecological model of behavior by testing a dynamic, person-environment interaction intervention. Findings will contribute a scalable mHealth protocol that can be integrated with school-based health initiatives.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel by integrating real-time GPS location with accelerometry to tailor sitting-break prompts, moving beyond static neighborhood assessments to dynamic, momentary context. It applies the JITAI framework, common in other health behaviors, specifically to disrupt sedentary behavior in the understudied adolescent population.

proposals.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Mood, Fatigue, and Context as Mediators of the Association Between Neighborhood Walkability and Device-Measured Sedentary Time in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: High levels of sedentary behavior (SB) in adolescents are a persistent public health concern, linked to adverse health outcomes. While pre-2026 studies by Sallis and others have established cross-sectional associations between neighborhood walkability and lower SB, the real-time psychological and contextual mechanisms driving these associations remain poorly understood. Understanding these momentary drivers is crucial for designing effective, targeted interventions that go beyond environmental changes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher neighborhood walkability is associated with lower device-measured sedentary time, and that this association is mediated by more frequent positive mood, lower fatigue, and more frequent exposure to social or outdoor contexts captured via Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) in daily life.
proposals.method: Approximately 200 adolescents (aged 12-16) will wear thigh-worn activPAL devices for 7 days to objectively measure sedentary time. Concurrently, they will complete EMA prompts 5 times per day on smartphones, reporting momentary mood (affect), fatigue, and context (location, social company, perceived environment). Neighborhood walkability will be calculated using geographic information systems (GIS) based on home addresses. Analyses will use multilevel structural equation modeling (MSEM) to test the hypothesized mediation pathways, accounting for the nested (moments within days within persons) data structure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, real-time evidence of the psychological and contextual pathways linking walkability to sedentary behavior in youth. It will move the field from identifying associations to explaining *how* environments influence behavior, offering specific, modifiable targets (e.g., mood regulation, fatigue management) for complementary behavioral interventions alongside environmental design.
proposals.novelty: While EMA has been used to study physical activity, its application to understand the *mechanisms* of environmental effects on sedentary behavior in adolescents is novel. This study uniquely integrates objective environment and behavior measures with intensive longitudinal subjective data to unpack the 'black box' of environment-behavior relationships.

proposals.title: Associations of Micro-Break Frequency and Duration with Cardiometabolic Risk in Adolescents: A Device-Based Study of Sedentary Bout Fragmentation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's work on walkability and device-measured activity, has established that prolonged, uninterrupted sitting is independently associated with adverse cardiometabolic outcomes, even among youth. However, the specific health impact of fragmenting sedentary time with very short, frequent standing or stepping breaks—a practical intervention for school settings—remains poorly understood. This study addresses a critical gap by moving beyond total sedentary time to examine the microstructure of sedentary behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a higher frequency of very short (≤30-second) standing/stepping breaks during prolonged sedentary bouts will be associated with more favorable cardiometabolic risk profiles (e.g., lower waist circumference, improved insulin sensitivity) in adolescents, independent of total moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.
proposals.method: We will recruit a diverse sample of 300 adolescents (aged 12-16). Participants will wear a thigh-worn activPAL4 accelerometer for 7 consecutive days to capture posture (sitting/lying, standing, stepping) with high temporal resolution. Using custom algorithms in R, we will identify sedentary bouts (≥5 minutes of sitting/lying) and quantify within-bout fragmentation by calculating the frequency and average duration of standing/stepping interruptions (micro-breaks). Cardiometabolic risk will be assessed via fasting blood draws (glucose, insulin, lipids), blood pressure, and anthropometrics. Associations will be tested using linear mixed models, adjusting for school, neighborhood walkability (via GIS), total sedentary time, and MVPA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence on whether micro-break patterns are a meaningful health-related dimension of sedentary behavior in youth. It will translate basic science on sedentary physiology into a measurable, real-world behavioral construct. Findings will directly inform the design of targeted school-based interventions to break up sitting time with feasible, high-frequency activity breaks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined longer breaks (e.g., 2-minute walks every 30 minutes), this study uniquely focuses on the health relevance of ultra-short, high-frequency interruptions that are more easily integrated into classroom routines. It applies high-resolution device-based measurement and bout fragmentation analysis—methods common in adult studies—to the understudied adolescent population within their key behavioral contexts of school and home.

proposals.title: Evaluating a Multi-Level Sit-to-Stand Intervention in Office and Community Settings: Associations with Device-Measured Sedentary Time and Physical Activity
proposals.motivation: Excessive sedentary time is a major, independent risk factor for chronic disease and mortality in adults. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's work on environmental correlates of activity, shows that individual-level interventions have limited reach and population impact. Scaling effective sit-to-stand strategies to entire buildings or community centers addresses this gap by modifying the physical and social environment to promote behavior change at a population level.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a multi-component sit-to-stand intervention across entire office buildings and community centers will be associated with a significant reduction in device-measured total sedentary time and an increase in light-intensity physical activity among participants, compared to control sites.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in 8 office buildings and 8 community centers. The intervention includes environmental modifications (installation of sit-stand desks/benches), organizational policies (standing meetings), and prompts (posters, digital alerts). The primary outcome is change in accelerometer/ inclinometer-measured total sedentary time and stepping time over 12 months. We will use multilevel linear regression models to test associations, accounting for clustering within sites and adjusting for individual-level covariates (age, sex, job role). Process evaluation will assess reach, fidelity, and contextual factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust evidence on the effectiveness and feasibility of scaling a sit-to-stand intervention for population-level impact. It will extend environmental health research from neighborhoods and schools into workplace and community-center settings, identifying specific, modifiable environmental and policy levers to reduce sedentary behavior in adults.
proposals.novelty: While prior IPEN and related studies have examined cross-sectional associations between neighborhood environments and activity, this proposal tests a scalable *intervention* in understudied institutional settings (workplaces/community centers), moving from observational ecology to experimental environmental change.

proposals.title: Predicting Long-Term Adherence to Sedentary Behavior Reductions in Adolescents: A Machine Learning Approach Using Acute Physiological Responses and Neighborhood Walkability
proposals.motivation: Despite known health risks, interventions to reduce adolescent sedentary behavior often show poor long-term adherence. Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others highlights the influence of neighborhood and school environments on activity, but lacks tools to identify individuals most likely to sustain behavioral changes. This gap limits the personalization and effectiveness of public health interventions aimed at increasing physical activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that short-term physiological responses (glucose and blood pressure) to a controlled sedentary bout, combined with individual and environmental factors (e.g., home/school neighborhood walkability), can be used to predict an adolescent's likelihood of long-term adherence to sedentary behavior reduction protocols.
proposals.method: We will recruit adolescents (n=200) and measure continuous glucose and blood pressure before, during, and after a standardized sedentary task. Device-measured sedentary time and stepping (activPAL) will be collected at baseline and over 6 months. Features will include physiological response curves (e.g., area under the curve, variability), baseline activity, and GIS-derived home/school neighborhood walkability (IPEN Adolescent methods). A stacked ensemble model (e.g., combining Random Forest, XGBoost, and a simple neural network) will be trained to classify 6-month adherence (dichotomized as maintaining ≥30-minute daily reduction in sedentary time). Model performance will be evaluated using repeated cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, physiology-informed predictive model for behavioral adherence, moving beyond static correlates. It will directly inform the personalization of school- and community-based activity interventions by identifying adolescents who may need additional environmental or social support. The work integrates Sallis's expertise in environmental correlates with emerging biobehavioral sensing and machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates environments with behavior, this study uniquely frames acute physiological reactivity as a potential *predictor* of long-term behavioral adherence. It applies an 'inverse problem' approach from systems physiology to a behavioral science question, combining high-frequency sensor data with ecological measures of neighborhood walkability in a predictive framework.

proposals.title: Acute Vascular and Metabolic Responses to Prolonged Sitting versus Interrupted Sitting in Adolescents: A Laboratory-Based Crossover Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 epidemiological work by Sallis and others has established strong associations between neighborhood walkability, active travel to school, and higher physical activity in adolescents. However, the acute physiological mechanisms linking sedentary behavior patterns, such as prolonged sitting common in school settings, to cardiometabolic risk in youth remain poorly understood. Moving from observational associations to controlled, mechanistic evidence is critical for designing targeted school-based interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, compared to a single prolonged sitting bout, frequent transitions from sitting to brief light-intensity activity will acutely improve postprandial vascular function (flow-mediated dilation) and substrate metabolism (glucose, insulin, triglycerides) in adolescents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, crossover laboratory study with 30 healthy adolescents (aged 13-15). Participants will complete two, 7-hour conditions on separate days: 1) PROLONGED SIT: uninterrupted sitting, and 2) INTERRUPTED SIT: sitting interrupted by 3-minute bouts of light-intensity walking every 30 minutes. Standardized meals will be provided. Primary outcomes are measured pre-, during, and post-condition: brachial artery flow-mediated dilation (FMD) via high-resolution ultrasound and metabolic markers via serial blood draws. Continuous femoral artery blood flow and popliteal artery shear rate will be monitored using Doppler ultrasound. Data will be analyzed using linear mixed-effects models with condition and time as fixed effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, mechanistic evidence of how breaking up sitting acutely affects vascular and metabolic health in adolescents, a key population for prevention. The findings will directly inform the physiological rationale for classroom-based 'activity break' interventions and translate Sallis's ecological research on environmental correlates of activity into a causal, physiological framework.
proposals.novelty: While prior lab studies have examined sitting interruptions in adults, this is novel in focusing on adolescents and employing a comprehensive, multi-system physiological assessment (vascular, hemodynamic, and metabolic) under tightly controlled dietary and activity conditions, isolating the acute effect of the behavioral pattern itself.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in Adolescent Physical Activity During COVID-19 Restrictions: Identifying Contexts and Populations with Increased Activity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research overwhelmingly reports that pandemic lockdowns decreased population physical activity (PA), especially among adolescents. However, this dominant narrative may obscure important heterogeneity. Identifying contexts where restrictions were associated with stable or increased PA is critical for understanding the complex interplay between environmental constraints, time use, and behavior, informing more resilient public health strategies for future disruptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents living in neighborhoods with higher pre-pandemic walkability and those who transitioned to remote schooling experienced different patterns of time reallocation, leading to subgroups with increased device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) or improved activity patterns (e.g., less sedentary fragmentation) during restrictions compared to pre-pandemic baselines.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal device-measured PA data (e.g., accelerometers) from existing adolescent cohorts with pre-pandemic (2019) and pandemic-period (2020-2021) measurements. Using GIS, we will characterize home neighborhood walkability (intersection density, land use mix) for each participant. We will employ finite mixture models (e.g., latent class growth analysis) to identify distinct trajectories of change in MVPA and sedentary patterns. Multinomial logistic regression will then test associations between trajectory membership and predictors: neighborhood walkability, school mode (in-person vs. remote), gender, and access to home exercise equipment.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond reporting average population declines to empirically identify subgroups of adolescents who maintained or increased PA during restrictions. It will directly test the moderating role of neighborhood walkability—a core theme of Sallis's work—in a disrupted behavioral context. Findings will contribute to a more nuanced evidence base for targeting interventions and designing built environments that support PA resilience.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in explicitly testing for positive deviance in PA during lockdowns, challenging the universal deficit model. It uniquely integrates the pre-pandemic built environment (walkability) with the pandemic-specific context of time reallocation (remote school) to explain heterogeneous behavioral responses, a synthesis not yet prominent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Testing Micro-Behavioral Nudges for Home-Based Physical Activity in Adolescents During COVID-19 Lockdowns: A Randomized Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic and associated lockdowns drastically reduced adolescents' physical activity (PA) by eliminating active travel to school and restricting access to community facilities. Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others established strong associations between neighborhood walkability, school environments, and PA, but these macro-level factors become irrelevant during mandated home confinement. There is an urgent need for evidence-based, hyper-localized interventions that can operate within the home environment to mitigate PA declines during public health emergencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents receiving contextually-timed, micro-behavioral nudges delivered via smartphone will demonstrate significantly higher levels of daily device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) and reduced sedentary time during a lockdown period, compared to a wait-list control group.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 adolescents (aged 13-17) and randomize them to intervention or control. The intervention uses a 2-week Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and nudging protocol via a smartphone app. The app uses a pre-programmed algorithm to deliver three types of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs): 1) time-based prompts (e.g., after 60 minutes of device-measured inactivity), 2) location-based prompts (triggered by being in a common home activity space like the living room), and 3) preference-based prompts (suggesting activities previously rated as enjoyable). PA and sedentary time will be measured concurrently using thigh-worn activPAL4 micro devices, which provide validated measures of stepping, standing, and sitting. Analyses will use multilevel modeling to test for group differences in daily stepping time and sedentary bout duration.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, rigorously evaluated protocol for micro-scale PA promotion that is independent of built environment features, directly addressing a critical gap in public health emergency preparedness. It will extend Sallis's work on environmental associations by testing a scalable, home-based behavioral intervention strategy, providing actionable knowledge for times when traditional activity settings are inaccessible.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel by shifting the intervention focus from the neighborhood or school scale down to the immediate home micro-environment and moment-to-moment behavior, using JITAIs informed by real-time activPAL data—a method not commonly combined in pre-2026 adolescent PA nudge studies focused on lockdown contexts.

proposals.title: The Temporal Dynamics of Walkability: Disentangling Weekday and Weekend Associations with Device-Measured Physical Activity in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's IPEN Adolescent studies, consistently links neighborhood walkability to higher physical activity (PA) using 7-day averages. However, these averages may obscure critical temporal variations. Adolescents' routines and environmental exposures differ starkly between structured school weekdays and unstructured weekends, potentially altering walkability-PA associations. Understanding these differences is crucial for designing temporally targeted interventions and refining built environment metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength of association between objective walkability indices and device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) and stepping will be significantly stronger on weekdays compared to weekends for adolescents, due to the mandatory destination of school and more predictable weekday routines.
proposals.method: We will re-analyze existing, pre-2026, pooled accelerometer and GPS data from international studies (e.g., IPEN Adolescent). Using GIS, we will calculate walkability indices (net residential density, land use mix, intersection density) for each participant's home neighborhood. We will employ multilevel linear regression models with interaction terms (walkability × day type: weekday/weekend) to test differential associations. The primary outcomes will be daily minutes of MVPA and step count, analyzed separately for weekdays and weekend days, adjusting for age, sex, and socioeconomic status.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond static, averaged associations by revealing how built environment impacts on adolescent PA are temporally contingent. It will provide evidence for whether walkability interventions should be universally promoted or specifically targeted to support weekday active travel. The findings will also inform the development of more nuanced analytical methods in environmental epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While day-of-week variation in PA is known, pre-2026 work has not rigorously tested if the *relationship* between walkability and PA itself varies by day type. This analysis challenges the standard practice of using 7-day averages as a homogeneous exposure metric, proposing a temporally explicit model of person-environment interaction.

proposals.title: Neighbourhood Walkability and Activity Substitution in Adolescents: A Compositional Analysis of Device-Measured Sedentary, Light, and Moderate-to-Vigorous Physical Activity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Sallis's IPEN Adolescent studies, consistently links higher neighbourhood walkability with increased moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA).
