However, the net effect on total daily activity energy expenditure and patterns remains unclear. If walkable environments encourage purposeful walking but simultaneously reduce non-exercise light activity (e.g., standing, incidental movement), the public health benefits may be overestimated, necessitating a more nuanced understanding of activity displacement.
proposals.hypothesis: Higher neighbourhood walkability is associated with a compositional shift in daily activity, characterized by increased MVPA and walking, but a proportional decrease in light-intensity activity, with no net change in sedentary time.
proposals.method: We will re-analyze existing, pre-2026, device-measured (accelerometer and inclinometer) activity data from adolescents in the IPEN Adolescent study (n~6000 across 5 countries). Using Geographic Information Systems (GIS), objective walkability indices (net residential density, intersection density, land use mix, retail density) will be calculated for each participant's neighbourhood. The primary analysis will use compositional data analysis (CoDA) techniques to model the entire 24-hour activity composition (sedentary, light, MVPA) as a function of walkability, adjusting for key covariates (age, sex, school location). Isotemporal substitution models will estimate the effect of substituting 10 minutes of one behaviour (e.g., light activity) with another (e.g., MVPA) at different walkability levels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first multi-country, compositional evidence on how walkability reshapes the entire activity profile in youth, moving beyond single-behavior associations. It will inform more accurate public health models by quantifying potential activity trade-offs and clarify whether walkability interventions primarily promote new activity or reorganize existing activity patterns.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant assumption that walkability is purely additive for activity, testing the novel hypothesis that it triggers a behavioral substitution from light activity to MVPA, a nuanced mechanism not previously examined in adolescent environmental health research.

proposals.title: Mapping Minute-by-Minute Contexts of Adolescent Physical Activity: A GPS-Accelerometer Fusion Study of Micro-Location and Walkability
proposals.motivation: While neighbourhood walkability is associated with higher physical activity in adolescents, existing studies aggregate data over days or weeks, obscuring the specific micro-locations (e.g., specific street segments, parks, home yards) where activity bouts occur. This aggregation limits our understanding of the precise environmental features that trigger or sustain active behaviours in real-time, hindering targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher walkability at the micro-location (minute-by-minute) level will be associated with a greater likelihood of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) bouts, and that these associations will be stronger for non-school, non-home locations compared to aggregated neighbourhood measures.
proposals.method: We will recruit adolescents (n=150) to concurrently wear high-frequency GPS loggers and tri-axial accelerometers for 7 days. GPS and accelerometer data will be fused and processed in 60-second epochs. Micro-locations will be classified using a GIS-based protocol (e.g., home, school, street, park, other). A machine learning model (Random Forest) will classify each epoch as sedentary, light, or MVPA. Walkability indices (using established IPEN methods) will be calculated for both the home neighbourhood and for a 50m buffer around each minute's GPS point. Multilevel logistic regression will model the odds of MVPA by micro-location walkability, controlling for time of day and individual factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, temporally linked evidence of how walkability at the immediate point of activity influences adolescent behaviour. It will move the field beyond residential neighbourhood effects to identify specific activity-promoting settings, offering concrete targets for micro-scale environmental redesign.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the minute-level spatiotemporal fusion of context and activity, shifting the unit of analysis from the home neighbourhood to the dynamic activity location, which is a significant advancement over cross-sectional area-level associations prevalent in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Gamifying the Journey to School: A Randomized Trial of a Location-Based Game to Increase Active Travel and Physical Activity in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Despite known benefits, many adolescents fail to meet physical activity guidelines. Active travel to school (ATS) is a key opportunity for habitual activity, but its prevalence is low. Prior work by Sallis and others shows neighbourhood walkability and parental perceptions influence ATS, yet scalable, engaging interventions that directly target adolescent motivation are underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: Adolescents assigned to use a gamified, location-based mobile application designed around their school journey will demonstrate higher levels of device-measured daily moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) and a greater frequency of active travel to school compared to a control group receiving standard activity information.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in 8 secondary schools. The intervention is a purpose-built mobile app employing core gamification elements: points for active travel, progress bars for weekly goals, and 'level-ups' unlocking narrative content. The app uses GPS and accelerometer data (via smartphone or linked device) to automatically verify trips and activity. Algorithms will award points based on trip distance (using GIS shortest-path routing) and MVPA minutes. The control app provides static maps and activity logs. Primary outcomes are device-measured daily MVPA and ATS frequency over a 4-week intervention period, analyzed using multilevel linear and logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide causal evidence on the efficacy of a theory-informed, technology-driven gamification intervention to increase adolescent physical activity. It will advance the field by testing a novel integration of behavioural economics (incentives via points) with ecological models (targeting a specific behaviour within the school-neighbourhood context), moving beyond correlational studies of environment and activity.
proposals.novelty: While gamification has been tested in general activity promotion, this proposal uniquely applies it to the specific, routine behaviour of the school journey, leveraging pre-2026 insights on environmental correlates of ATS to design context-aware game mechanics that bridge home and school neighbourhoods.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Impact of a Multi-Component Public Health Messaging Campaign on Adolescent Physical Activity: A Natural Experiment in School Neighborhoods
proposals.motivation: Despite established physical activity guidelines, most adolescents do not meet them, with significant public health consequences. Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others shows that environmental factors like neighborhood walkability and school context are strongly associated with activity levels, yet population-level interventions like mass media campaigns, proven effective for smoking cessation, have been understudied for promoting physical activity guidelines among youth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a theory-based, multi-channel public health messaging campaign targeting adolescents will increase awareness of physical activity guidelines and, when implemented in neighborhoods with higher walkability, will lead to a measurable increase in device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) compared to control neighborhoods.
proposals.method: We will conduct a natural experiment in 20 matched school neighborhoods (10 intervention, 10 control). The intervention is a 6-month campaign using social media, school-based materials, and local media, grounded in the Theory of Planned Behavior and focusing on guideline awareness and local active opportunities. Pre- and post-campaign, we will survey adolescents (n=~2000) on guideline awareness and attitudes and use accelerometers (ActiGraph) on a subsample (n=~400) to measure MVPA. Primary analysis will use multilevel linear regression, testing the interaction between campaign exposure and neighborhood walkability (audited using IPEN Adolescent methods) on changes in MVPA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel evidence on the real-world efficacy of a guideline-promotion campaign and its interaction with the built environment. It will advance the field by moving beyond observational associations to test an intervention that bridges individual messaging and environmental determinants, offering practical insights for public health policy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes environmental correlates of activity, this study innovatively applies a proven public health tool (mass media campaigning) specifically to activity guideline promotion and tests its synergistic effect with neighborhood walkability, a novel integration of communication and ecological approaches.

proposals.title: Associations between neighborhood walkability, school travel time, and adherence to physical activity guidelines in adolescents: A population-scale analysis using consumer wearable data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, population-level monitoring of physical activity (PA) guidelines relies on infrequent self-report surveys, which are prone to bias and lack temporal resolution. The widespread adoption of consumer wearables (e.g., Fitbit, Garmin) presents an unprecedented opportunity for scalable, objective, and longitudinal PA surveillance. This is critical for adolescents, as their PA patterns are influenced by complex interactions between built environments like neighborhood walkability and daily schedules, including school travel time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher neighborhood walkability and shorter school travel times will be associated with a higher probability of adolescents meeting the WHO physical activity guidelines (≥60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous PA daily), as measured objectively via consumer wearable data.
proposals.method: We will partner with a major wearable data aggregator (e.g., Evidation Health, Fitabase) to obtain de-identified minute-level step and heart rate data for a geographically diverse cohort of adolescents (aged 12-17). Adherence to guidelines will be classified using validated cut-points for consumer devices (e.g., >100 steps/minute for MVPA). We will link participant home neighborhoods (census block groups) to established walkability indices (e.g., IPEN Adolescent walkability score) and calculate school travel time via GIS network analysis. Multilevel logistic regression will model guideline adherence as a function of walkability, travel time, and their interaction, adjusting for individual and area-level covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, device-based evidence on how built environment features jointly influence guideline adherence in adolescents. It will demonstrate a novel methodology for leveraging commercial wearable data for public health surveillance, moving beyond small research-grade accelerometer studies. Findings will directly inform policies targeting school siting and neighborhood design to promote adolescent health.
proposals.novelty: While Sallis's prior IPEN Adolescent work used research-grade devices in limited samples, this proposal scales to a population level by harnessing existing consumer wearable ecosystems. It uniquely integrates continuous device data, detailed built environment metrics, and school travel time to provide a more holistic, real-world assessment of activity determinants.

proposals.title: Neighbourhood walkability, adolescent sleep quality, and cardiometabolic risk: A mediation analysis testing sleep as the primary pathway
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's IPEN Adolescent work, robustly links neighbourhood walkability to adolescent physical activity and health. However, the dominant causal model prioritizes physical activity as the primary mediator for Non-Communicable Disease (NCD) risk reduction. Emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests built environments also influence stress and sleep, which are independent, potent risk factors for adolescent cardiometabolic dysfunction. This study inverts the model to test if sleep improvement is a stronger, more direct pathway from walkability to reduced NCD risk in adolescents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) higher neighbourhood walkability will be associated with better adolescent sleep quality and shorter sleep latency, and (2) the association between walkability and improved cardiometabolic risk profiles (e.g., BMI, waist circumference, HOMA-IR) will be significantly mediated by these sleep metrics, with a stronger mediation effect than that observed for device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA).
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis of 500 adolescents (aged 12-17) across diverse neighbourhoods in a major metropolitan area, leveraging GIS-derived walkability indices (net residential density, intersection density, land use mix). Sleep quality and latency will be measured via 7-day wrist-worn accelerometry (ActiGraph wGT3X-BT) using validated algorithms (e.g., Sadeh). Cardiometabolic risk will be assessed via fasting blood draws and anthropometry. We will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test a parallel mediation model, comparing the standardized path coefficients and proportion mediated for the sleep pathway versus the MVPA pathway, controlling for key covariates like school location, parental perceptions, and home environment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel evidence for sleep as a critical mechanistic pathway linking built environment to adolescent health, moving beyond the physical activity-centric model. It will inform more holistic public health and urban planning interventions aimed at NCD prevention by identifying modifiable environmental features that support restorative sleep.
proposals.novelty: While prior Sallis research established walkability-activity associations, this proposal innovatively positions sleep quality as a potential primary driver of health benefits, directly testing its mediating role against the established MVPA pathway within the same analytical framework for adolescents.

proposals.title: Personalizing School-Based Physical Activity: A Pilot Study of Adaptive Algorithms for Real-Time Dance Intensity Modulation in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Despite known benefits, many adolescents do not meet physical activity (PA) guidelines. School-based interventions are crucial, but one-size-fits-all approaches often fail to sustain engagement. Wearable sensors and adaptive algorithms from educational technology, which personalize learning, offer a novel, data-driven method to personalize PA delivery in real-time, potentially increasing adherence and effectiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive algorithm, using real-time wearable sensor feedback to personalize dance exercise intensity and duration, will result in higher levels of sustained moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) and greater self-reported enjoyment among adolescents during school-based sessions, compared to a standard, non-adaptive protocol.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pilot RCT in a school setting. Adolescents will be randomized to an adaptive or control group during dance sessions. The adaptive group will use a system where a waist-worn accelerometer (e.g., ActiGraph) streams data to a tablet-based application. A rule-based algorithm, inspired by Knowledge Tracing models from EdTech, will classify real-time PA intensity. Based on a participant's recent MVPA history and a target zone, the algorithm will dynamically suggest changes in dance movement intensity or brief rest periods via auditory cues from the instructor's tablet.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel methodological framework for real-time PA personalization, bridging concepts from educational technology and public health. It will provide initial evidence on the feasibility and acute effects of adaptive PA programming in a key setting (schools) for a critical population (adolescents), informing the design of next-generation, scalable interventions.
proposals.novelty: While sensor-based activity monitoring is common, applying adaptive personalization algorithms from EdTech to dynamically modulate the *delivery* of structured PA in real-time is a novel translational approach. It moves beyond descriptive associations of environment and activity to an interactive, closed-loop intervention model.

proposals.title: A Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention to Reduce Stress and Sedentary Time in Adolescents: Integrating Ecological Momentary Assessment with Contextual Walkability
proposals.motivation: Adolescents experience high stress and increasing sedentary time, both linked to poor health outcomes. While Dr. Sallis's pre-2026 work shows neighbourhood and school walkability are associated with general physical activity, these static environmental factors do not address moment-to-moment opportunities for stress reduction and activity breaks. There is a critical need for dynamic, personalized interventions that operate within adolescents' daily contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) delivering prompts for brief dance breaks during periods of high stress or prolonged sedentarism, as detected via ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and accelerometry, will be more effective at reducing stress and increasing light-intensity activity in adolescents than generic activity prompts, and that intervention effectiveness will be moderated by home and school neighbourhood walkability.
proposals.method: We will recruit adolescents (n=120) and use a micro-randomized trial design. Participants will wear a research-grade accelerometer (ActiGraph) and complete EMA surveys on a smartphone 4 times daily for 4 weeks, reporting stress and context. A JITAI algorithm will trigger a dance break prompt when two conditions are met: 1) EMA-reported high stress OR accelerometer-detected >30 min of sedentary time, and 2) participant is in a pre-defined 'available' context (e.g., not in class). The algorithm will use a reinforcement learning component to adapt prompt timing based on individual historical response rates. Home and school neighbourhood walkability will be calculated using pre-2026 GIS methods (e.g., IPEN Adolescent study protocols).
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, context-aware JITAI model for adolescent stress and sedentary behaviour management. It will advance the field by empirically testing how dynamic, within-person states (stress, sedentarism) interact with static environmental features (walkability) to influence the efficacy of a mobile health intervention, moving beyond cross-sectional associations to causal micro-temporal processes.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates JITAI and EMA methods with Dr. Sallis's established research on environmental correlates of activity, testing walkability not as a direct predictor but as a moderator of a dynamic behavioural intervention's success. The use of a reinforcement learning-augmented algorithm for timing activity prompts based on individual responsiveness is a novel technical advancement in adolescent physical activity research.

proposals.title: A School-Based, Context-Aware Intervention to Reduce Sedentary Time in Adolescents: The Stand & Move Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sallis's IPEN Adolescent work, shows neighborhood and school environments are strongly associated with physical activity. However, sedentary behavior is a distinct health risk factor not simply addressed by promoting activity. Adolescents spend over 60% of their waking hours sedentary, with schools being a major contributor. An intervention explicitly targeting the reduction and breaking of sedentary time, independent of increasing moderate-to-vigorous activity, is a critical gap in public health.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-level school-based intervention that provides real-time, context-aware prompts to break sitting will significantly reduce device-measured total and prolonged sedentary time in adolescents, compared to a wait-list control group, without increasing their overall physical activity levels.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in 8 secondary schools. Participants will wear thigh-worn activPAL4 devices for 7 days at baseline, 3 months, and 9 months. The intervention integrates two components: 1) Environmental modifications (e.g., standing desks in classrooms, marked standing zones) based on pre-2026 walkability principles applied to interior design, and 2) A just-in-time adaptive intervention (JITAI) delivered via a smartphone app. The JITAI algorithm will use activPAL data streamed via Bluetooth to detect prolonged sitting bouts (>20 mins). It will then deliver a personalized prompt (vibration + message) to stand, with prompt timing algorithmically tailored to avoid disrupting academic instruction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first rigorous tests of a 'sedentary behavior only' intervention in youth, generating novel evidence on its efficacy and mechanisms. It will advance the field by applying ecological models and device-based measurement specifically to sedentary behavior reduction, and by developing a feasible, context-aware JITAI protocol for use in school settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sallis and others has focused on environmental correlates of activity, this intervention flips the paradigm to explicitly target sedentary time as the primary outcome. The novelty lies in combining static environmental changes with a dynamic, algorithm-driven JITAI that responds to real-time sedentary behavior within the complex context of the school day.

proposals.title: Associations between neighbourhood walkability and cardiovascular health in adolescents with physical activity contraindications: a cross-sectional study
proposals.motivation: A core assumption in built environment research is that walkability benefits health primarily by increasing physical activity. This assumption fails for adolescents with conditions where physical activity is medically contraindicated (e.g., severe cardiac conditions, post-operative recovery) or ineffective for primary management. Understanding whether walkable environments confer health benefits through non-activity pathways (e.g., reduced stress, social connection, independent mobility) is crucial for equitable, inclusive public health planning.
proposals.hypothesis: Among adolescents for whom vigorous physical activity is contraindicated or not a primary management strategy for cardiovascular risk, higher neighbourhood walkability will be associated with better cardiovascular health indicators (e.g., lower resting heart rate, better heart rate variability, lower perceived stress) independently of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA).
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis of a pre-existing cohort (e.g., leveraging data from IPEN Adolescent or similar studies) enriched with clinical recruitment to identify adolescents with physician-diagnosed conditions contraindicating typical MVPA. Primary exposures are objective GIS-based walkability indices (net residential density, land use mix, intersection density). Outcomes are device-measured cardiovascular indicators (7-day heart rate monitoring via ActiHeart) and self-reported stress (Perceived Stress Scale). We will use generalized linear mixed models, adjusting for sociodemographics, school environment, and home environment. The key analysis will test the interaction between walkability and MVPA, expecting a significant association between walkability and cardiovascular health in the low/contraindicated MVPA group.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence on whether walkable neighbourhoods support cardiovascular health in youth through mechanisms beyond facilitating physical activity. It will challenge the dominant activity-centric framework and inform more nuanced public health guidelines and urban design policies that consider the needs of populations with limited mobility or activity restrictions.
proposals.novelty: This directly relaxes the foundational treatment assumption in walkability-health research by focusing on a population where physical activity is not the presumed mediator, shifting the focus to psychosocial and autonomic pathways. It applies Sallis's established neighbourhood and school environment frameworks to a novel clinical sub-population.

proposals.title: Cross-Sector Economic and Health Benefits of School-Active Transport Policies: A Simulation Study Integrating GPS, Health, and Air Pollution Data
proposals.motivation: Policies promoting active transport to school (ATS) are typically justified for increasing adolescents' physical activity, but their broader co-benefits for air quality, traffic congestion, and long-term health are rarely quantified. This limits comprehensive policy evaluation and cross-sectoral advocacy. Pre-2026, advances in device-based measurement (GPS, accelerometry) and spatial analytics allow for novel integration of mobility, environmental, and health data to model these systemic impacts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a policy-induced increase in ATS mode share will generate significant, monetizable benefits from reduced vehicle emissions (improving air quality) and traffic congestion, alongside established health gains from increased physical activity, with benefits varying by neighbourhood walkability.
proposals.method: We will conduct an agent-based simulation using data from an existing cohort of adolescents (e.g., IPEN Adolescent study) with device-measured activity and GPS traces. The simulation will model shifts from car trips to active trips under policy scenarios, using established algorithms (e.g., MATSim) to estimate changes in local traffic volume and emissions (using MOVES or similar models). Reductions in PM2.5/NO2 will be linked to health outcomes via concentration-response functions. Physical activity gains will be monetized using disability-adjusted life-year (DALY) and healthcare cost frameworks. Economic benefits of reduced congestion will be estimated using value-of-time and fuel cost savings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, integrated valuation model for ATS policies, moving beyond single-outcome evaluations. It will deliver concrete, monetized estimates of cross-sector benefits to inform transportation, health, and environmental policy planning. The findings will strengthen the economic case for investing in walkable school neighbourhoods and active travel infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sallis and others has established associations between neighbourhood walkability and ATS, this study innovatively integrates mobility simulation with environmental and health economic modelling to quantify and monetize the broader societal co-benefits of a specific behaviour change policy, a methodological advance in the field.

proposals.title: Integrating Neighborhood Walkability Assessments into Clinical Physical Activity Protocols for Adolescent Hypertension and Diabetes Management
proposals.motivation: Physical activity (PA) is a cornerstone of non-communicable disease (NCD) management, yet clinical guidelines often provide generic advice, ignoring the environmental context of patients. Pre-2026 evidence shows neighborhood walkability strongly influences habitual PA, particularly in adolescents. For adolescents with hypertension or type 2 diabetes, a mismatch between clinical PA prescriptions and their unsupportive home/school environments likely contributes to poor adherence and suboptimal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Clinical PA protocols for adolescents with hypertension or diabetes that are explicitly integrated with a standardized assessment of home and school neighborhood walkability will lead to greater increases in device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity and improved clinical biomarkers (e.g., blood pressure, HbA1c) compared to standard care protocols.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in clinical settings. The intervention will develop and test an integrated protocol where clinicians use a tablet-based tool combining the validated NEWS (Neighborhood Environment Walkability Scale) for youth and GIS-derived walkability indices (e.g., intersection density, land-use mix) to map a patient's home and school environs. Algorithms will cross-reference this environmental profile with the patient's daily schedule to generate personalized, contextually feasible PA recommendations (e.g., active travel routes, after-school activity locations). Control groups receive standard PA counseling. Primary outcomes are 6-month changes in accelerometer-measured PA and clinical markers.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, standardized clinical tool that operationalizes ecological models of health behavior by directly linking environmental psychology (walkability perceptions) and objective built-environment data with personalized medicine. It will provide the first evidence-based framework for creating spatially-informed PA prescriptions, moving beyond one-size-fits-all guidelines to improve real-world adherence and health outcomes in adolescent NCD management.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sallis's prior work established associations between walkability and behaviors, this study innovatively translates those findings into a clinical intervention tool. It moves from observational epidemiology to experimental implementation, embedding environmental assessment directly into the clinical workflow for chronic disease management, a novel integration of public health and clinical practice.

proposals.title: Global Monitoring of Adolescent Physical Inactivity: A Multi-Country Analysis of Trends, Built Environment Correlates, and NCD Burden Projections
proposals.motivation: Physical inactivity is a leading global risk factor for non-communicable diseases (NCDs), yet consistent, device-based monitoring of population trends, especially for adolescents, is lacking. Pre-2026, initiatives like the Global Matrix and IPEN studies provide snapshots, but a harmonized, longitudinal surveillance system integrating built environment data is needed to inform urban and school policy for chronic disease prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that between 2015 and 2025, global trends in device-measured physical activity among adolescents will show significant geographic and socioeconomic disparities, and that these trends will be strongly associated with changes in neighborhood walkability and school environment policies.
proposals.method: We will establish a global monitoring consortium to harmonize and analyze existing device-measured accelerometry data (e.g., from IPEN Adolescent, iHealth, other cohorts) from over 20 countries. Using a standardized data processing pipeline (e.g., GGIR open-source software), we will calculate consistent metrics of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) and sedentary time. Trends will be analyzed using multi-level growth models. These activity trends will be spatially linked via GIS to temporally matched built environment data (walkability indices, school location) and correlated with World Health Organization NCD burden estimates using longitudinal panel regression models.
proposals.contribution: This will create the first open-access, longitudinal dataset of harmonized, device-measured physical activity in adolescents across diverse global contexts. It will directly quantify how changes in the built environment correlate with population activity trends, providing an evidence base for evaluating global NCD prevention targets and urban planning interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes cross-sectional associations, this proposal innovates by analyzing *trends* in device-measured activity and their *temporal* linkages to built environment changes and NCD burdens at a global scale, moving beyond single-country or snapshot studies.

proposals.title: Evaluating the impact of targeted urban safety interventions on active travel to school among adolescents: A longitudinal, quasi-experimental study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence consistently shows that perceived safety is a critical barrier to adolescents' active travel (walking/cycling) to school, a key source of daily physical activity. While general neighbourhood walkability is associated with higher activity, specific, modifiable safety features (e.g., traffic calming, improved lighting, pedestrian crossings) require targeted evaluation to inform cost-effective urban planning. This research addresses a gap in moving from cross-sectional associations to testing causal impacts of concrete safety interventions on adolescent mobility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the implementation of a bundled urban safety intervention (traffic calming, enhanced pedestrian infrastructure, and improved street lighting) in school catchment areas will lead to a significant increase in the prevalence and frequency of active school travel among adolescents, mediated by improvements in both objective and perceived safety.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, quasi-experimental design with matched control neighborhoods. Objective active travel will be measured using GPS and accelerometer devices (e.g., ActiGraph) over 7-day periods at baseline and 12-month follow-up. Perceived safety and neighbourhood environment will be assessed via validated surveys (e.g., Neighborhood Environment Walkability Scale for Youth). The primary analysis will use multilevel mixed-effects regression models, adjusting for individual and household covariates, to compare changes in device-measured active travel minutes and trips between intervention and control groups. Mediation analysis will test whether changes in perceived safety explain the intervention effect.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, longitudinal evidence on the causal efficacy of a specific set of urban safety interventions for promoting adolescent active travel. It will advance the field by linking concrete planning strategies directly to device-measured behavioral outcomes, offering actionable guidance for municipal policymakers. The findings will extend Sallis's IPEN Adolescent and iHealth work by testing an intervention derived from its correlational findings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the quasi-experimental test of a multi-component safety intervention, using pre-post device-based measurement of active travel specifically in the school journey context. This moves beyond cross-sectional associations of walkability to a causal evaluation of a defined urban planning strategy.

proposals.title: Nudging Movement: A Randomized Field Experiment Testing Choice Architecture Interventions to Increase Incidental Physical Activity in Adolescents' School and Neighborhood Environments
proposals.motivation: Despite known associations between neighborhood walkability and physical activity, adolescents' activity levels remain low. Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others has established environmental correlates but has not sufficiently tested low-cost, scalable interventions derived from behavioral economics to actively change behavior within existing environments. This gap limits practical, evidence-based strategies for public health and urban planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically designed choice architecture nudges (e.g., point-of-decision prompts, gamified stairwells, optimized default routes) implemented in school and adjacent neighborhood settings will lead to a significant increase in device-measured incidental physical activity (stepping) among adolescents, compared to control conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in 8 secondary schools. Schools will be randomized to receive a suite of nudge interventions or serve as controls. Interventions will target specific choice points (e.g., staircase vs. elevator entrances, active vs. passive lunchtime queues, walking route signage). The primary outcome is change in daily stepping minutes, measured objectively over 7 days using thigh-worn activPAL accelerometers pre- and post-intervention. Analyses will use multilevel linear mixed models to account for clustering within schools and adjust for covariates like baseline activity and perceived environment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence on the efficacy of behavioral nudges for increasing adolescent physical activity in real-world settings. It will translate correlational findings on walkability into a testable intervention framework, offering concrete, low-cost tools for schools and municipalities. The results will bridge behavioral economics with environmental health science, advancing theory on how subtle environmental redesign influences habitual activity.
proposals.novelty: While Sallis's prior work excelled at measuring associations between environments and activity, this proposal actively manipulates micro-features of those environments using principles from behavioral economics (nudge theory), moving from observation to experimental intervention. It applies a novel theoretical lens (choice architecture) to the well-studied problem of adolescent physical activity promotion.

proposals.title: Digital Phenotyping of Adolescent Mobility: Using Smartphone Sensors to Unpack the Temporal and Contextual Associations of Walkability with Physical Activity
proposals.motivation: Understanding how neighbourhood walkability influences adolescent physical activity (PA) is crucial for public health, yet existing studies rely on self-report or limited accelerometer data, lacking contextual detail. The rise of mobile health (mHealth) and digital phenotyping, using ubiquitous smartphone sensors, offers an unprecedented opportunity to passively and continuously measure real-world PA and its precise environmental context. This addresses a key gap in Sallis's prior IPEN Adolescent and iHealth studies by capturing the dynamic interplay between time, location, and activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher neighbourhood walkability, measured objectively via geographic information systems (GIS), will be associated with greater smartphone-sensed active travel and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) in adolescents, and that these associations will be strongest during key time windows (e.g., before/after school) and when contextual sensor data (e.g., location, audio) confirms the individual is in their neighbourhood.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 adolescents (aged 13-17) for a 7-day protocol. Smartphone sensors (GPS, accelerometer, microphone) will collect continuous passive data. A validated machine learning pipeline will be employed: 1) GPS data will be geofenced to classify location contexts (home, school, neighbourhood). 2) Accelerometer data will be processed using a random forest classifier (trained on lab-calibrated activities) to identify activity modes (walking, running, vehicle travel, stationary). 3) Time-stamped, context-labeled activity bouts will be merged with GIS-based walkability indices (net residential density, land use mix, intersection density) for each participant's home neighbourhood. Multilevel models will test associations, with time-of-day and location context as moderators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, ecologically valid evidence of how walkability influences device-measured PA patterns across time and space in adolescents. It will advance methods in health geography by integrating passive sensing with built environment metrics. The findings will directly inform targeted urban planning and school travel interventions by identifying the specific times and contexts where environmental improvements could yield the greatest PA benefits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sallis and others has used GPS or accelerometers separately, this study's novelty lies in the fully passive, multi-sensor digital phenotyping approach that fuses location, activity, and time to create a dynamic behavioural context, moving beyond static 'place-based' associations to model 'person-context' interactions throughout the day.

proposals.title: Global Burden of Cardiovascular Disease Attributable to Occupational Sedentariness: A Comparative Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: While Dr. Sallis's pre-2026 work has extensively examined how neighborhood and school environments influence physical activity, particularly in adolescents, the built environment for adults is heavily shaped by the workplace. Occupational sedentariness is a pervasive, policy-relevant exposure, yet its specific, quantifiable contribution to the global cardiovascular disease (CVD) burden remains unclear. Estimating this burden is critical for shifting the focus from individual behavior change to systemic, occupational health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant and growing proportion of global CVD incidence (e.g., ischemic heart disease, stroke) is attributable to prolonged occupational sitting, independent of leisure-time physical activity, and that this burden varies substantially by country income level and occupational sector.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comparative risk assessment (CRA) analysis, following established Global Burden of Disease Study frameworks. We will synthesize pre-2026 meta-analytic data on the relative risk of CVD outcomes associated with high occupational sitting versus active occupations. These risk estimates will be combined with global labor force data (from the International Labour Organization) on the prevalence of sedentary occupations across countries and demographic groups. We will use DisMod-MR 2.1, a Bayesian meta-regression tool, to model exposure distributions and ensure consistency. Population Attributable Fractions (PAFs) will be calculated and applied to GBD 2019 CVD incidence estimates to derive disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) lost.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first global, sector-specific estimates of CVD burden due to occupational sedentariness, moving beyond individual-level associations to a population-health perspective. It will directly inform international occupational health guidelines and corporate wellness policies by quantifying the health cost of sedentary work, creating a parallel evidence base to that for active transportation and recreational activity.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the typical physical activity research lens—from promoting 'active' environments to quantifying the health cost of 'sedentary' ones—and applies Dr. Sallis's expertise in environmental correlates of activity to the understudied occupational domain on a global scale.

proposals.title: Identifying the Minimum Effective Dose of Physical Activity for Cardiovascular Risk Reduction in Adolescents: A Multi-Contextual Analysis of School, Home, and Neighborhood Environments
proposals.motivation: Current physical activity guidelines for adolescents are broad, and adherence is low. A 'minimum effective dose' approach could provide more achievable, personalized targets, potentially increasing compliance. This is critical for cardiovascular risk reduction, as pre-2026 evidence shows even small increases in activity can confer health benefits, but the precise thresholds within real-world environmental contexts remain unclear.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there are identifiable, context-dependent thresholds of device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) associated with clinically meaningful reductions in cardiovascular risk factors (e.g., blood pressure, lipid profiles) in adolescents, and that these thresholds are moderated by the walkability of their neighborhood and school environments.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis of existing cohort data (e.g., IPEN Adolescent, iHealth) linking accelerometer-derived activity patterns (MVPA minutes/day) with cardiovascular biomarkers. Using piecewise regression and Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve analysis, we will identify potential inflection points where increased activity yields diminishing returns for risk reduction. We will then employ multilevel moderation models to test if these dose-response relationships are stronger in adolescents from high-walkability neighborhoods or schools with supportive active travel policies.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond generic associations to propose evidence-based, context-sensitive activity thresholds, directly informing more precise public health messaging. It will integrate Sallis's expertise on environmental correlates with dose-response physiology, offering a pragmatic framework for targeting interventions to subgroups who may benefit most from small, achievable increases in activity.
proposals.novelty: While dose-response curves exist, this study uniquely frames the question as a 'dual problem' of minimal activity for maximal benefit and explicitly tests how built environments (neighborhood and school walkability) modify these fundamental physiological relationships, a novel integration of behavioral epidemiology and environmental science.

proposals.title: Associations between digital walkability cues, time-use patterns, and physical activity in adolescents with elevated blood pressure: A fully remote mHealth intervention trial
proposals.motivation: Hypertension management in adolescents increasingly requires scalable, accessible interventions. Pre-2026 research by Sallis and others established strong associations between neighborhood walkability and physical activity, but these environmental factors are not modifiable for individuals. Digital delivery can overcome this by simulating supportive cues and providing structured remote guidance, a crucial step for reaching at-risk youth in low-walkability areas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fully remote, app-based physical activity program, which uses personalized digital prompts based on time-use patterns and simulated walkability cues, will lead to higher levels of device-measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity and improved blood pressure control in adolescents with elevated blood pressure, compared to a digital education-only control.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month RCT with 200 adolescents (ages 13-17). The intervention group uses a custom app employing a reinforcement learning algorithm to deliver context-aware activity prompts by analyzing individual time-use patterns from device data (e.g., after-school hours, weekend mornings). The app integrates GPS and Google Street View APIs to provide location-based 'digital walkability' cues (e.g., highlighting nearby green spaces). Activity is measured via accelerometry and blood pressure via mailed home monitors. Analyses will use multilevel models to test associations between prompt adherence, activity changes, and blood pressure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel evidence on the efficacy of a fully digital, environmentally informed PA intervention for adolescent hypertension. It will extend Sallis's work on walkability associations by testing if digitally delivered environmental cues can modify behavior. The findings will inform scalable mHealth strategies for non-communicable disease prevention in youth.
proposals.novelty: This integrates the well-studied construct of neighborhood walkability into a fully remote intervention via digital cues, moving from observational associations to experimental modification. It uniquely applies a reinforcement learning approach to personalize prompts based on continuously sensed time-use patterns, a novel method in adolescent PA promotion.

ideas.title: Decomposing the Walkability Composite: A Principal Component and Mediation Analysis Approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a composite walkability score is the optimal operationalization by adapting a solution from nutritional epidemiology. In that field, researchers address the masking problem of composite dietary scores by using principal component analysis to derive patterns from individual food data, followed by causal mediation analysis to pinpoint which specific nutrients mediate the pattern-health outcome relationship. Adapted to urban health, the research would first apply PCA to standard built environment metrics (e.g., intersection density, land use mix, retail density, park proximity) to identify core urban form patterns. Subsequently, causal mediation models would test hypotheses about whether a pattern's association with an activity outcome (e.g., utilitarian walking) is significantly driven by a single component like intersection density, thereby revealing the specific mechanistic drivers hidden by the composite score.

ideas.title: Apply Experience Sampling with Context-Aware Triggering to Walking Perceptions
ideas.description: This idea adapts a method from psychophysiology where physiological event detection triggers ecological momentary assessment. Instead of using biosensors, the smartphone's own inertial sensors are programmed to passively detect the start of a walking bout. When a bout is detected, and its location is cross-referenced with a GIS-based walkability index, the system automatically delivers an EMA prompt to the user. This context-aware triggering ensures real-time perceptual data (e.g., stress, enjoyment, perceived safety) is captured precisely during the activity and within the specific environmental context, solving the problem of recall bias and misalignment between prompts and activity episodes.

ideas.title: City-wide School Walkability Index using Ecological Health Assessment Framework
ideas.description: This idea scales up a school walkability audit to a city-wide benchmarking system by drawing an analogy from ecological health assessments. In ecology, scientists evaluate complex systems (like forests or watersheds) by measuring standardized bioindicators at multiple sites and compiling them into a comparative index to gauge overall health and guide restoration. Adapted to this context, the research would define a core set of infrastructural and safety 'walkability indicators' to be measured uniformly around every public school in a city. The collected data would be synthesized into a composite School Walkability Support Index for each school, creating a city-wide comparative benchmark. This index would function like an ecological report card, enabling the identification of schools in 'walkability deserts,' the prioritization of infrastructure investments, and the longitudinal tracking of the urban environment's support for active travel, thereby applying a proven framework for systemic assessment from environmental science to urban planning and public health.

ideas.title: Crowdsourced Path Network Validation Using GPS-Linked Stepping Bouts
ideas.description: This research integrates crowdsourced geographic data (e.g., from OpenStreetMap) with high-fidelity activPAL and GPS data from free-living participants. By algorithmically linking recorded stepping bouts to specific pedestrian paths and destinations identified in the crowdsourced map, the method can identify discrepancies—such as mapped paths that see no actual use (suggesting inaccuracies or barriers) or frequently used routes that are missing from maps. This generates a validated, behaviorally-informed pedestrian network layer that directly links infrastructure data to real-world walking performance, useful for urban planning and public health research.

ideas.title: A Multi-Modal, Dynamic Data Integration System for Auditing Experiential Street-Level Walkability
ideas.description: This research designs a system that overcomes the static limitations of imagery-only audits by integrating computer vision analysis with dynamic, experiential data streams. It combines perceptual features from street view imagery with real-time traffic sensor data, historical pedestrian incident reports, and anonymized movement data to model the actual pedestrian experience across different times and conditions. The output is a temporally-variable walkability score that reflects both the built environment and the dynamic behavioral and sensory context, providing a more robust and safety-aware tool for urban planners.

ideas.title: Sequence-Optimized Predictive Audit (SOPA) for Youth Mobility
ideas.description: This research fuses sequence analysis and audit tool development to create a novel, data-driven method for generating abbreviated neighborhood audits. First, sequence analysis is applied to GPS and time-use data to classify whole-day activity profiles of children, specifically identifying profiles where active commuting to school is embedded within other supportive routines. Second, a predictive modeling approach (e.g., machine learning) is used to identify which specific audit items (e.g., sidewalk quality, crossing safety, land-use mix) are most strongly associated with these optimal, activity-rich sequences, rather than just the binary outcome of active commuting. The result is a Sequence-Optimized Predictive Audit (SOPA)—a brief, field-ready tool whose items are empirically validated for predicting not just a single behavior, but the complex daily activity patterns that support sustainable, healthy mobility in youth.

ideas.title: Participatory Ground-Truthing: Co-Designing Micro-Scale Accessibility Audits with Sensor-Augmented Lived Experience
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond expert audits by creating a participatory framework where older adults and disabled individuals are active co-researchers. It equips participants with simple sensor kits (e.g., on walkers or wheelchairs) to log objective data like vibrations and slopes, paired with a real-time, low-burden app for subjective feedback on perceived safety and difficulty. This dual-stream data is fused to create dynamically updated 'lived experience maps' that identify and prioritize discrepancies between technical standards and practical usability, driving iterative, community-validated redesign of micro-scale routes and audit criteria.

ideas.title: Adapt Swarm Intelligence Rules from Ecology to Model Pedestrian Activity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to modeling collective animal behavior from ecology to the context of urban walkability. In swarm intelligence models, simple, local interaction rules (alignment, cohesion, separation) for individual agents, combined with environmental gradients and agent heterogeneity, generate complex emergent group patterns. This analogous solution is adapted by defining human pedestrian agents with heterogeneous attributes (e.g., age, trip purpose, mobility) and programming them with analogous local movement rules—such as goal attraction (to destinations), path following (on sidewalks), and personal space maintenance (crowd avoidance). These rules are then explicitly modulated by quantifiable features of the built environment that define 'walkability' (e.g., intersection density, land-use mix, sidewalk width). By simulating the bottom-up interactions of these agents within a detailed virtual environment, the model can generate and test hypotheses about emergent population-level activity patterns (peak flows, route distributions, amenity usage) under varied urban design scenarios, providing a mechanistic link between street-level design and neighborhood-scale activity.

ideas.title: Apply genomic sequence alignment to classify daily activity motifs and link to urban form
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from bioinformatics to the sociological study of daily routines. In genomics, multiple sequence alignment (MSA) algorithms are used to compare DNA sequences, identify conserved motifs, and classify sequences into functional families. Here, we apply analogous computational alignment techniques to time-use diary data, treating whole-day activity sequences as strings in a 'behavioral alphabet.' The goal is to align sequences from many individuals to discover prevalent, shared activity motifs (e.g., 'compressed errand runs' or 'prolonged home-based periods'). These classified daily 'genotypes' can then be modeled as outcomes to determine how specific motifs are associated with the walkability and built environment features of the individual's neighbourhood, providing a novel, pattern-based link between urban form and the structure of daily life.

ideas.title: Testing self-selection against network-constrained accessibility using longitudinal audit data
ideas.description: This research integrates GIS network service area analysis with longitudinal, parcel-level built environment audit scores to create dynamic measures of objective neighborhood accessibility. It then employs a longitudinal study design, tracking individuals who move into or out of these carefully audited, network-defined areas to test the self-selection hypothesis. By comparing changes in active commuting behavior before and after a move against the precise, audited accessibility characteristics of both origin and destination neighborhoods, the study can disentangle the effects of self-selection from true environmental influence. This provides a robust, quasi-experimental test of whether improved accessibility causes behavioral change or merely attracts already active residents.

ideas.title: Triangulated Child-Centric Environmental Audits via Biosensor-Augmented, Contextualized Walks
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond simple child-reported scores by creating a robust, multi-source methodology for assessing child-perceived safety and walkability. It integrates data from three concurrent streams: child-led narrative walks with think-aloud protocols, wearable biosensors capturing physiological stress responses, and GPS-tracked environmental sensors. These streams are fused and analyzed in a developmentally-sensitive framework, using photo-elicitation interviews to retrospectively ground and validate the child's narrative. The output is a contextualized, composite audit score that is both perceptually grounded and empirically validated, overcoming the unreliability of standalone self-report.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Audit Micro-Environments for Vulnerable Pedestrians
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Ecology and Conservation Biology—the use of Species Distribution Models (SDMs) to map suitable habitats based on environmental variables—to the problem of auditing micro-scale pedestrian environments for older adults or disabled individuals. The analogous solution involves defining a species' niche requirements and projecting them onto a landscape. Here, we adapt this by first rigorously defining the 'niche' or set of necessary and sufficient micro-environmental features (affordances like curb cuts, bench availability, and constraints like excessive slopes or uneven surfaces) for safe mobility of a target demographic. The audit tool is then adapted to systematically survey an urban area, collect data on these key variables, and generate a granular 'navigability suitability map' that highlights safe routes and pinpoints hazardous zones, effectively modeling the 'habitat suitability' for vulnerable pedestrians.

ideas.title: A behaviorally-calibrated multi-criteria model for estimating likely active commuter populations
ideas.description: This research develops a robust model to estimate the population likely to engage in active commuting, moving beyond simple physical proximity. It integrates traditional GIS network service area analysis with granular behavioral, socioeconomic, and perceptual data layers—such as commute mode choice surveys, crime safety perceptions, and household vehicle availability—to create a stratified 'commute propensity' index. The system is calibrated and validated using targeted survey data linked to specific audit locations, allowing it to weight the accessible population within a network service area by their probability of actual use. The final output provides planners with a more realistic and actionable estimate of potential active commuters for a given facility or corridor.

ideas.title: Barrier-Centric Universal Design Audit (B-UDA): A diagnostic tool for systemic exclusion in pedestrian networks
ideas.description: This research develops a novel Barrier-Centric Universal Design Audit (B-UDA) tool. It inverts the standard supportive-features audit to instead diagnose, catalog, and severity-score the micro-scale environmental barriers that actively hinder safe and independent travel for specific vulnerable pedestrian groups, such as older adults or individuals with mobility, vision, or cognitive impairments. By fusing a barrier-focused lens with a multi-group vulnerability framework, the tool maps not just isolated obstacles but the interconnected sequences of barriers that create impassable routes or 'exclusion pathways' for different users. The output provides planners with a prioritized, equity-focused intervention map, targeting barrier removals that yield the greatest net improvement in network usability across multiple vulnerable populations.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Audit Street-Level Walkability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method of ecological niche modeling from biogeography to the urban analysis context. In ecology, models like MaxEnt predict species distribution by learning the environmental conditions associated with known species occurrences. Here, we treat locations with verified high walkability as 'presence points' and use computer vision to extract a suite of perceptual features (e.g., sidewalk width, street enclosure, visual complexity, pedestrian infrastructure) from street-view imagery as the 'environmental layers'. A model is trained to learn the multivariate visual signature of walkability from these labeled examples. It can then be applied to large-scale street-view datasets to generate a continuous, audit-quality walkability probability map for entire cities, providing a fine-grained, perceptual measure derived directly from the pedestrian's visual experience.

ideas.title: Fusing GPS and activPAL data using sensor fusion techniques from robotics
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the problem of linking specific stepping bouts to walkable destinations by drawing an analogy from robotics. In autonomous navigation, robots solve the analogous problem of determining their precise location and movement state by fusing data from multiple imperfect sensors (e.g., IMUs for fine movement, GPS for global position). The analogous solution is the use of sensor fusion algorithms like Kalman or particle filters. Adapted to this research context, the solution is to develop and apply these fusion techniques to synchronously integrate the high-fidelity stepping bout data from the activPAL with the contextual GPS location data. This creates a probabilistically estimated, continuous movement path, allowing researchers to more accurately attribute each bout of walking not just to a final destination, but to a specific route and environmental context, significantly enriching the analysis of behavior in built environments.

ideas.title: Adapting Ethnobotanical Knowledge Elicitation for Child-Centric Urban Audits
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that objective, adult-led audits are sufficient for assessing child-perceived safety and walkability. By drawing an analogy from ethnobotany—where researchers successfully elicit valid, structured ecological knowledge from children using participatory, age-appropriate methods—we adapt its solution to urban research. The adapted research idea employs child-centric techniques like guided 'walk-and-talk' neighborhood tours, participatory mapping using drawings and symbols, and gamified reporting tools to systematically collect and codify children's perceptual data. This methodology transforms subjective child experiences into validated, auditable scores, providing a rigorous alternative to the external observer audit and prioritizing the child's perspective as a legitimate source of urban knowledge.

ideas.title: Developing a predictive subscale suite for active commuting to school using machine learning feature selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from bioinformatics, where machine learning feature selection is used to distill complex genomic data into a minimal predictive biomarker panel for disease diagnosis. The analogous problem in active commuting research is the need to shorten lengthy audit tools for practical field use. The adapted research idea involves applying algorithms like LASSO regression or Random Forest to a rich dataset containing many potential audit variables (e.g., sidewalk quality, crossing safety, traffic speed, perceived safety). The model's objective is to predict the outcome of active commuting rates. The variables (audit items) assigned the highest importance by the model form the core of a new, abbreviated audit version. This method ensures the shortened tool is empirically validated for predictive accuracy, moving beyond expert opinion to create a data-optimized field instrument.

ideas.title: Agent-based simulation of pedestrian behavior dynamically calibrated by computer vision streetscape audits
ideas.description: This research integrates computer vision-based streetscape auditing with agent-based modeling to create a high-fidelity simulation platform for pedestrian behavior. Automated image segmentation of street-level imagery provides granular, geotagged data on sidewalk quality, crossing infrastructure, and other MAPS-SRTS subscales to empirically define the 'rules' and environmental constraints within the agent-based model. Heterogeneous agents with different attributes (e.g., age, mobility preference) then navigate this realistic environment, generating simulated activity patterns. This allows for virtual experiments to predict how targeted improvements to specific streetscape features, identified by the audit, would alter population-level walking and route choices under various scenarios.

ideas.title: Integrating GIS Network Analysis with Weighted Audit Scores via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodological framework of Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) from ecology to urban mobility research. In SDMs, a species' potential range is modeled as a function of environmental suitability layers. Analogously, the 'suitability' for active commuting can be modeled by integrating two core spatial datasets: 1) a network service area defining temporal accessibility (e.g., 15-minute walk/bike sheds from origins like homes), and 2) a spatially continuous surface of infrastructure quality derived from audit scores (e.g., sidewalk condition, street connectivity, safety). The adapted solution creates a composite, weighted 'Active Commuting Suitability Index' for any point in space, allowing researchers to model the accessible population not just by count, but weighted by the quality of the accessible network. This moves beyond binary accessibility to model gradations of access quality, identifying areas where populations are nominally 'served' but by low-quality infrastructure.

ideas.title: Disentangling Contextual Variance from Measurement Error: A Multi-Method Reliability Assessment of MAPS-SRTS Across Geographic Settings
ideas.description: This study systematically isolates measurement reliability from true environmental differences to assess the MAPS-SRTS tool's performance across diverse contexts. Using a hybrid design, raters from suburban, rural, and non-US settings first score a common set of standardized video audits to establish a pure inter-rater reliability benchmark. They then conduct field audits in their own contexts on segments matched to the video audits on key built environment features. By comparing agreement on standardized versus in-context audits and analyzing variance components, the study quantifies the instrument's reliability independent of geographic setting and identifies any context-specific biases in rater perception or tool application.

ideas.title: Micro-Environmental Path Auditing via Single-Cell Transcriptomics Approach
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from single-cell biology, where scRNA-seq analyzes individual cells to understand specific states within a complex tissue. Similarly, we propose to 'scale down' urban route auditing to the level of a single household-to-school path. The adapted solution involves creating a tool that audits the specific path from a single child's home to their school entrance by treating it as a discrete unit of analysis. This tool would capture a high-resolution 'profile' of that specific route—including micro-environmental factors like pavement condition at precise points, localized traffic patterns, hyper-local crime data, and personalized accessibility barriers—much like sequencing reveals a single cell's unique gene expression. This allows for personalized route safety assessments and interventions, moving beyond aggregate district-level data to address the precise realities of an individual's journey.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Streetscape Auditing for Unpredictable Dynamic Environments
ideas.description: This research designs a streetscape auditing system that moves beyond scheduled temporal sampling to directly address unpredictable environmental volatility. It establishes a framework for continuous monitoring of multi-source urban data streams to automatically detect anomalous events that impact pedestrian and cyclist accessibility. Upon detecting a significant disruption, the system triggers an immediate, tailored audit protocol to capture the real-time conditions and their evolution. This approach ensures auditing resources are focused on actual dynamic failures rather than assumed temporal patterns, producing a more responsive and operationally relevant assessment of streetscape performance.

ideas.title: Temporal Auditing of Streetscapes via Ecological Succession Monitoring Methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the static audit assumption in streetscape analysis by adapting methods from ecology for monitoring temporal dynamics. In ecology, scientists study ecosystem succession through repeated, cyclical sampling at fixed locations to track change over time. The analogous solution is adapted by establishing permanent 'urban transects' for temporal auditing of streetscapes. Data collection is synchronized to key travel times (e.g., commute peaks, school hours) using sensors and observations to measure dynamic variables like congestion, pedestrian flows, and space usage. This approach generates time-series data that reveals how streetscape conditions and functionality evolve, distinguishing predictable daily/weekly cycles from long-term trends, thereby providing a dynamic rather than static understanding of urban environments.

ideas.title: Adapting flocking algorithms to simulate children's commuting route choices under environmental constraints
ideas.description: This idea adapts agent-based modeling solutions from the field of collective animal behavior, specifically models of flocking and schooling. In ecology, these models generate complex navigation patterns from simple rules where agents react to neighbors and environmental gradients. The analogous solution is adapted by modeling child agents with parameters for peer influence (mimicking alignment/cohesion) and by using neighborhood environmental audit scores (e.g., safety, walkability) to create a spatial 'fitness landscape' that attracts or repels agents. The simulation framework would test how variations in audit parameters and social weighting influence the emergent distribution of route choices and group travel patterns during school commutes.

ideas.title: Dynamic Agent-Based Modeling of Children's Route Choice Calibrated with Real-Time Crowdsourced Path Data
ideas.description: This research develops an agent-based modeling (ABM) framework where simulated child agents make route-choice decisions based on traditional environmental audit scores. Crucially, the model is dynamically calibrated and validated using a continuous stream of anonymized, crowdsourced geographic data (e.g., from mapping applications) that reveals actual pedestrian paths, speeds, and frequent avoidance behaviors. This synergy allows the simulation to move beyond static 'what-if' scenarios to model how route choices evolve in response to real-time conditions and temporary obstacles, creating a powerful tool for predicting the impact of infrastructure changes on children's mobility and identifying latent safety or connectivity issues.

ideas.title: A Reverse-Engineering Protocol for Built Environment Design Based on MAPS-SRTS Audit Targets
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'inverse design' solution from structural biology and protein engineering to create a generative tool for policy and urban design. In the analogous field, computational algorithms solve the inverse problem of finding amino acid sequences for a desired protein structure, rather than predicting structure from sequence. Applying this logic, the project will develop a manual and potentially a software protocol that acts as an inverse function for the MAPS-SRTS audit tool. Users (e.g., planners, engineers) will specify a target audit score or a performance goal for a specific audit domain (e.g., 'achieve a score of 3 for pedestrian crossings'). The system will then output the necessary and sufficient combinations of built environment features—defined with engineering and design specifications—required to reliably achieve that target, thereby scaling the translation of research-based audit scores into actionable, prescriptive design guidance.

ideas.title: Apply a 'Core Sample' Audit Protocol to Document Built Environment Evolution
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core sampling methodology from paleoclimatology to document built environment changes. In sedimentology, scientists extract and analyze layered cores to reconstruct historical environmental shifts. The adapted research idea establishes permanent, geo-tagged photo stations ('core points') along streets targeted for infrastructure intervention. Using the MAPS-SRTS instrument's audit categories, researchers would collect standardized visual data from these identical points at regular intervals before, during, and after interventions. This creates a longitudinal 'varve record' of the streetscape, enabling precise auditing of the sequence, pace, and physical evolution of modifications, much like analyzing sediment layers to date and characterize past climatic events.

ideas.title: Bias-Modeled, Multi-Source Data Fusion for Equitable Path Infrastructure Auditing
ideas.description: This redesigned system proactively addresses the inherent biases of crowdsourced geographic data by modeling the coverage and contributor biases of each source (e.g., Strava, OpenStreetMap, social media). It uses these models to identify 'audit priority zones'—areas likely suffering from data voids—and directs targeted field audits there. A contextual data fusion engine then integrates traditional audit data, biased VGI, and purposefully collected community-sourced data (e.g., from organized local walks) by weighting each stream based on its estimated reliability and representativeness for the specific location and path type. This results in a more equitable and robust map of connectivity and conditions that corrects for, rather than amplifies, digital divides.

ideas.title: Audit Educational Environments Using Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that an educational audit tool validated primarily in urban elementary schools is universally applicable. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where niche modeling is used to predict species presence in unstudied areas, the solution is to treat the tool's current validation setting as its known 'niche.' By defining the contextual variables of that niche, researchers can then strategically sample and apply the audit in contrasting environments like rural middle/high schools. This methodical expansion maps the tool's boundaries and limitations, revealing biases and generating insights specific to non-urban and non-elementary settings.

ideas.title: Active Learning Framework for Adaptable and Equitable Streetscape Auditing
ideas.description: This system uses computer vision for initial streetscape scoring but is designed to overcome geographic and contextual bias. It employs an active learning loop to automatically identify locations where the model's predictions are unreliable due to unfamiliar visual features. These high-uncertainty areas are then prioritized for human verification, and the resulting validated data is used to continuously retrain and adapt the core model. This approach ensures the audit system becomes more accurate and equitable across diverse environments, rather than perpetuating errors from a static training set.

ideas.title: Crowdsourced Path Auditing with Citizen Science Quality Control Protocols
ideas.description: This idea adapts quality control protocols from citizen science biodiversity monitoring (e.g., eBird, iNaturalist) to the problem of validating crowdsourced geographic data on path connectivity and conditions. The analogous solution uses a multi-tiered validation system combining automated filters, expert review, and contributor reputation scoring. Adapted to this context, the research would develop a framework where volunteered path data from mapping applications is processed through automated anomaly detection (e.g., for improbable GPS traces), weighted by a contributor reputation system built from historical accuracy, and supplemented with a protocol for expert or local volunteer verification of flagged entries. This creates a hybrid audit method that leverages crowdsourcing scale while maintaining data quality standards suitable for formal infrastructure assessment.

ideas.title: Adapting Geospatial Spectral Analysis for Multi-Scale Streetscape Feature Detection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodology from remote sensing—using spectral libraries and targeted indices for automated land cover classification—to the problem of automated streetscape auditing. In remote sensing, solutions analyze pixel spectra against known material signatures to map features at geographic scale. Adapted to computer vision for streetscapes, the research would involve building a library of visual 'spectral-spatial' signatures for urban materials and objects (sidewalk concrete, road asphalt, vegetation, crosswalk paint) from street-level imagery. It would then develop specialized visual indices, analogous to agricultural or hydrological indices in satellite analysis, to reliably detect and quantify features relevant to MAPS-SRTS subscales. This approach aims to transfer the robustness and scalability of geospatial classification techniques to the fine-grained, ground-level audit context, potentially improving automated scoring for features defined by specific material properties and spatial arrangements.
